figures.name: Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair
figures.title: Khalidi Princess
figures.roleDescription: Mother of Saudi royalty
figures.significance: She was a Khalidi princess who became the mother of Prince Turki and King Saud of Saudi Arabia.
figures.biography:
Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair was a princess from the Al 'Ura'yir family, which was the ruling clan of the Bani Khalid tribe that controlled eastern Arabia. The Al 'Ura'yir were part of the Al Humaid branch that traditionally held chieftainship of the Bani Khalid confederation.

She became historically significant through her marriage into the Saudi royal family, becoming the mother of both Prince Turki and King Saud. This connection represents an important matrimonial alliance between the Bani Khalid rulers and the emerging Saudi state, despite the two powers being political rivals during much of the 18th and early 19th centuries. Her lineage connected the ancient ruling families of eastern Arabia with the founders of modern Saudi Arabia.
achievements: Mother of King Saud of Saudi Arabia
Mother of Prince Turki

figures.name: Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah
figures.title: Founder of Al Sadd Sports Club
figures.roleDescription: Founder and early leader of Qatar's most successful football club
figures.significance: Founded Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969, which became Qatar's most successful football club with 64 official championships.
figures.biography:
Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah was the principal founder of Al Sadd Sports Club, established on 21 October 1969 in Doha, Qatar. He was born and raised in the Al Sadd district of Doha, which gave the club its name. The Al-Attiyah family members excelled in football but did not wish to join any of the existing football clubs at the time.

After consulting with Jassim bin Hamad Al Thani, who was the minister of Youth and Sports, Ali bin Hamad Al-Attiyah and his relatives decided to create their own club. They presented their application and club statute to Abdulaziz Buwazair, the operating manager of the Supreme Sports Committee, resulting in the formation of Al Sadd Sports Club. In the club's initial year, Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah oversaw the club's performance and made crucial decisions while his relative Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah coached the team.

The club's initial popularity was owed in part to its affiliation with the Al Attiyah tribe, described as a prominent local tribe. Under his leadership and vision, Al Sadd went on to become the most successful sports club in Qatar, winning their first official league title in 1973-74 and eventually accumulating a national record of 64 official football championships.
achievements: Founded Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969
Established Qatar's most successful football club
Led club to its first league championship in 1971-72

figures.name: Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah
figures.title: First Coach of Al Sadd Sports Club
figures.roleDescription: First coach and founding member of Al Sadd Sports Club
figures.significance: Served as the first coach of Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969 and was part of the founding Al Attiyah family group.
figures.biography:
Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah was one of the founding members of the Al Attiyah family group that established Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969. He served as the club's first coach during its inaugural year of establishment, when the team trained on a football pitch in a local high school in Doha.

While his relative Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah oversaw the club's overall performance and made crucial decisions as the founder, Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah took on the practical role of coaching the team. This division of responsibilities between the Al Attiyah family members helped establish the organizational structure that would contribute to the club's early success and eventual dominance in Qatari football.
achievements: First coach of Al Sadd Sports Club
Founding member of Al Sadd Sports Club
Helped establish the club's early organizational structure

figures.name: Khalid bin Mohammad Al Attiyah
figures.title: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs and Deputy Prime Minister
figures.roleDescription: Qatari politician who served in senior government positions
figures.significance: Served as Qatar's Minister of Foreign Affairs from 2013-2016 and Deputy Prime Minister until 2024, representing a prominent political figure from the Al Attiyah family.
figures.biography:
Khalid bin Mohammad Al Attiyah was born on 9 March 1967 in Qatar. His family belongs to the Banu Tamim tribe, the same tribe as Qatar's ruling Al Thani family. His father was notably the founder of the Qatar Armed Forces, establishing the Al Attiyah family's significant role in Qatar's military and political development.

Al Attiyah received extensive education, earning a bachelor's degree in air science from King Faisal Air Academy in 1987 and a law degree from Beirut Arab University in 1993. He later obtained a master's degree in public law (1991) and a PhD in law (2006) from Cairo University. He began his career as a fighter pilot in Qatar's air force from 1987 to 1995, then established a law firm in 1995.

His political career advanced significantly when he served as minister of state for international cooperation from 2008 to 2011, followed by minister of state for foreign affairs from 2011. In June 2013, he was appointed as Minister of Foreign Affairs, replacing Hamad bin Jassim Al Thani. In January 2016, he became Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of State for Defense Affairs, serving until November 2024. Throughout his career, he also held various board positions and served as president of the National Committee for Human Rights from 2003 to 2008.
achievements: Minister of Foreign Affairs of Qatar (2013-2016)
Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of State for Defense Affairs (2016-2024)
Fighter pilot and Qatar Armed Forces officer (1987-1995)
PhD in Law from Cairo University
President of National Committee for Human Rights (2003-2008)

figures.name: Sheikha Aisha bint Hamad Al Attiyah
figures.title: Wife of Former Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.roleDescription: Mother of former Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
figures.significance: Mother of Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, who served as Emir of Qatar from 1995 to 2013, representing the Al Attiyah family's connection to the ruling family.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Aisha bint Hamad Al Attiyah was one of the wives of Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, who served as Emir of Qatar from 1972 to 1995. She was the mother of Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa, who would later become Emir of Qatar from 1995 to 2013 after deposing his father in a bloodless coup.

Her marriage to Sheikh Khalifa represented an important alliance between the Al Attiyah family and the ruling Al Thani family. Through her son Hamad bin Khalifa, the Al Attiyah family maintained significant influence in Qatar's political landscape, as Hamad bin Khalifa became one of Qatar's most transformative rulers, modernizing the country and establishing it as a major regional power.
achievements: Mother of Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
Connected Al Attiyah family to ruling Al Thani dynasty

figures.name: Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah
figures.title: Wife of Deputy Emir Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.roleDescription: Engineer and wife of Qatar's Deputy Emir
figures.significance: Married to Qatar's Deputy Emir Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani in 2012, representing continued Al Attiyah family connections to the ruling family.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah is an accomplished engineer who earned her degree from the Qatar University College of Engineering and Computer Science. She is the daughter of Sheikh Muhammad bin Abdullah bin Hamad Al Attiyah, who served as Premier and Chairman of the Administrative Control and Transparency Authority.

On 22 March 2012, she married Sheikh Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani at Al Wajbah Palace. Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani is the Deputy Emir of Qatar and unofficial heir presumptive to the throne, having been appointed to this position by his half-brother Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani in November 2014. Her marriage represents the continuation of strong ties between the Al Attiyah family and Qatar's ruling Al Thani family, maintaining the family's influential position in Qatari society.
achievements: Engineering degree from Qatar University
Married to Deputy Emir of Qatar
Daughter of former Premier of Qatar

figures.name: Nasser Al-Attiyah
figures.title: Rally Driver and Dakar Rally Winner
figures.roleDescription: Professional rally driver and Olympic shooter
figures.significance: Won the 2011 Dakar Rally alongside German co-driver Timo Gottschalk, becoming the first Qatari to win this prestigious motorsport event.
figures.biography:
Nasser Al-Attiyah is a prominent Qatari rally driver who achieved international recognition by winning the 2011 Dakar Rally. He partnered with German co-driver Timo Gottschalk for this historic victory, representing a significant achievement for Qatari motorsport.

Al-Attiyah first competed in the Dakar Rally in 2010 alongside Gottschalk, where they won four stages and finished as runners-up to Volkswagen teammates Carlos Sainz and Lucas Cruz, losing by just over two minutes. The following year in 2011, Al-Attiyah and Gottschalk returned with determination and assumed the lead of the rally after victory in the eighth stage. When problems befell their main competitors Sainz and Cruz in the eleventh stage, Al-Attiyah and Gottschalk built a commanding lead of over 50 minutes that they maintained until the end of the rally, securing Qatar's first Dakar Rally victory.
achievements: Winner of 2011 Dakar Rally
Runner-up in 2010 Dakar Rally
Won four stages in 2010 Dakar Rally
First Qatari to win Dakar Rally

figures.name: Noora bint Hathal Al Dosari
figures.title: Third Wife of the Emir of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Qatari royal and spouse of Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.significance: She is the third wife of the current Emir of Qatar and mother to four of his children.
figures.biography:
Noora bint Hathal Al Dosari married Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, the Emir of Qatar, on 25 February 2014. She became his third wife, after Sheikha Jawaher bint Hamad bin Suhaim Al Thani and Sheikha Al-Anoud bint Mana Al Hajri. Together they have four children: Sheikh Joa'an bin Tamim (born 27 March 2015), Sheikh Mohammed bin Tamim (born 17 July 2017), Sheikh Fahad bin Tamim (born 16 June 2018), and Sheikha Hind bint Tamim (born 5 February 2020).

In February 2023, Al Dosari accompanied her husband on an official visit to Spain, where she visited the Nasrid palaces of Alhambra. As a member of the Qatari royal family through marriage, she participates in official state functions and diplomatic visits alongside the Emir.
achievements: Mother to four children of the Emir of Qatar
Participates in official state visits

figures.name: Muna bint Jassim Al Hassan Al Dosari
figures.title: Wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.roleDescription: Mother of fourteen children to a former Qatari Foreign Minister
figures.significance: She was the wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, Qatar's Foreign Minister, and mother to nine sons and five daughters.
figures.biography:
Muna bint Jassim Al Hassan Al Dosari was the wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad bin Abdullah bin Jassim bin Muhammed Al Thani, who served as Qatar's Foreign Minister from February 1972 until his death in August 1985. She was the mother of all fourteen of his children - nine sons and five daughters. Her daughters included Muna bint Suhaim, Rudha bint Suhaim, Amna bint Suhaim, Al Anoud bint Suhaim, and Muneera bint Suhaim.

After her husband's sudden death from a heart attack in August 1985, her sons blamed Ghanim Al Kuwari, the minister of information and culture, for not responding promptly to their father's calls for medical attention. The sons were subsequently imprisoned after they attempted to assassinate Al Kuwari, demonstrating the family's significant influence and the political tensions within Qatar's ruling circles at the time.
achievements: Mother to fourteen children of a prominent Qatari political figure

figures.name: Yasser Al-Dosari
figures.title: Imam of Masjid al-Haram
figures.roleDescription: Senior Islamic cleric and Imam at Islam's holiest mosque
figures.significance: He serves as Imam of Masjid al-Haram in Mecca, one of the most prestigious religious positions in Islam.
figures.biography:
Yasser Al-Dosari is a prominent Islamic scholar who serves as Imam of Masjid al-Haram, the Great Mosque of Mecca, which is considered the most significant mosque in Islam and encloses the Kaaba. He was appointed as Imam in 2019 and as Khatib (preacher) in 2022, making him one of the leading religious figures in the Islamic world.

As Imam of Masjid al-Haram, Al-Dosari leads prayers for millions of pilgrims who visit Mecca annually for Hajj and Umrah. The mosque is the main setting for these pilgrimages and serves as the focal point for over 5 million Muslims who perform the Hajj every year. His position places him among the most influential religious leaders in Saudi Arabia and the broader Islamic community, as he delivers sermons and leads prayers at Islam's holiest site.
achievements: Appointed Imam of Masjid al-Haram in 2019
Appointed Khatib of Masjid al-Haram in 2022
Leads prayers for millions of pilgrims annually

figures.name: Sami Jasem Al-Dosari
figures.title: Saudi Arabian footballer
figures.roleDescription: Professional football player
figures.significance: He is a Saudi Arabian footballer born in 1965, representing the Al Dosari family in professional sports.
figures.biography:
Sami Jasem Al-Dosari is a Saudi Arabian footballer who was born in 1965. As a member of the prominent Dawasir tribe, he represents the family's presence in professional sports in Saudi Arabia. His career coincided with the development of football in the Kingdom during the late 20th century.

While specific details about his club career and achievements are not extensively documented in the available research, his inclusion among notable members of the Dawasir tribe indicates his significance within Saudi Arabian football circles. The Dawasir tribe has a strong presence throughout the Gulf region and has produced notable figures across various fields including politics, religion, and sports.

figures.name: Mariam Adhbi Al Jalahma
figures.title: Physician and Politician
figures.roleDescription: Physician, civil servant, and politician who became one of the first female members of Bahrain's Consultative Council
figures.significance: She was one of the first female members of Bahrain's Consultative Council and later became chief executive of the National Health Regulatory Authority.
figures.biography:
Mariam Adhbi Al Jalahma was born in Bahrain in 1961 and completed her secondary education at Al Hoora Secondary School for Girls in 1977. She studied for a bachelor of medicine and surgery at the Qasr El Eyni faculty of Cairo University, graduating in 1985. She worked in the paediatric department of the Bahrain Defence Force Hospital in 1986–87, before joining the family medicine training programme of the Ministry of Health, where she worked until 1990.

After earning a certificate of specialisation in family medicine from the American University of Beirut in 1990, Al Jalahma worked as a family medicine consultant at Muharraq from 1990 to 1993. From 1993 to 1994 she was deputy chief medical officer at the National Bank of Bahrain's health centre. In 1994 she was awarded a higher diploma in medical education from the University of Dundee School of Medicine. From 1996 she held an assistant professorship at the Faculty of Medicine of the Arabian Gulf University, and in 1999 she also began introducing health programmes on radio and television.

In 2000 Al Jalahma completed a diploma in healthcare management at the Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland. In the same year she was one of four women appointed to the Consultative Council by Emir Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, becoming its first female members. She remained on the Consultative Council until 2002, after which she became Coordinator of International and Public Relations at the Ministry of Health. Al Jalahma was appointed to the executive board of the Gulf Cooperation Council Health Ministers' Council in 2007. In 2015 she was appointed chief executive of the National Health Regulatory Authority.
achievements: First female member of Bahrain's Consultative Council in 2000
Chief executive of the National Health Regulatory Authority from 2015
Executive board member of the Gulf Cooperation Council Health Ministers' Council from 2007
Assistant professor at the Faculty of Medicine of the Arabian Gulf University from 1996

figures.name: Jaber Al Jalahma
figures.title: Tribal Chief
figures.roleDescription: Chief of the Al Jalahma family who was killed by Al Khalifa forces
figures.significance: He was the chief of the Al Jalahma family who was killed during conflicts over revenue distribution in Zubarah.
figures.biography:
Jaber Al Jalahma was the chief of the Al Jalahma family during the 18th century when they were part of the Bani Utbah tribal confederation in Zubarah. The Al Jalahma family, allied under the Bani Utbah tribe, had joined Al Khalifa in Zubarah after migrating from Kuwait, leaving Al Sabah as the sole proprietors of Kuwait.

However, disagreements arose between the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma over the distribution of revenues from their activities in Zubarah. This led Al Jalahma to move to Ar Ru'ays, just east of Zubarah. The conflict escalated when Al Khalifa attacked them there and killed their chief Jaber Al Jalahma, marking a significant moment in the internal struggles within the Bani Utbah confederation.

figures.name: Rahmah bin Jabir Al Jalahma
figures.title: Leader and Naval Commander
figures.roleDescription: Leader of Al Jalahma clan based in Khor Hassan who opposed Al Khalifa through piracy and military alliances
figures.significance: He was a prominent opponent of Al Khalifa rule, conducting piracy against their ships and supporting their enemies until his death in a dramatic battle in 1826.
figures.biography:
Rahmah bin Jabir Al Jalahma was the leader of the Al Jalahma clan based in Khor Hassan during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. He held a deep grudge against the Al Khalifa family, likely stemming from earlier conflicts within the Bani Utbah confederation, including the killing of his predecessor Jaber Al Jalahma by Al Khalifa forces.

Rahmah bin Jabir actively opposed Al Khalifa rule through maritime warfare and political alliances. He pirated their ships and consistently supported their enemies in various conflicts. His opposition was significant enough to pose a continuous threat to Al Khalifa authority and trade interests. His resistance came to an end in 1826 when he was killed in what contemporary sources described as a dramatic battle, finally removing a persistent thorn in the side of Al Khalifa rule.
achievements: Led sustained maritime resistance against Al Khalifa rule
Maintained Al Jalahma autonomy in Khor Hassan

figures.name: Lulwa bint Abdulla bin Sulayman Al Jalahma
figures.title: Shaikha
figures.roleDescription: Second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, ruler of Bahrain
figures.significance: She was the second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who ruled Bahrain from 1932 to 1942, representing a marriage alliance between the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma families.
figures.biography:
Shaikha Lulwa bint Abdulla bin Sulayman Al Jalahma was a member of the prominent Al Jalahma family who became the second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who would later become the ruler of Bahrain from 1932 to 1942. Her marriage to Hamad represented a significant political alliance between the Al Khalifa ruling family and the Al Jalahma family, which had historically been both allies and rivals within the broader Bani Utbah tribal confederation.

This marriage alliance was part of the complex web of relationships that characterized Gulf politics in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, where intermarriage between prominent families served to strengthen political bonds and resolve historical grievances. Through her marriage to the future ruler, she became part of the ruling household during a crucial period in Bahrain's history, including the discovery of oil and the modernization of the country's administration.

figures.name: Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan
figures.title: First President of the UAE and 16th Ruler of Abu Dhabi
figures.roleDescription: Founding father and first president of the United Arab Emirates
figures.significance: Founded the United Arab Emirates and served as its first president from 1971 until his death in 2004.
figures.biography: Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan was the founding father and first president of the United Arab Emirates, serving from the federation's establishment in 1971 until his death in 2004. He was also the 16th ruler of Abu Dhabi, having become emir in 1966. He had multiple wives and many children, including his sons Khalifa, Mohamed, Mansour, and others who would go on to play key roles in UAE leadership. Zayed was instrumental in transforming the UAE from a collection of tribal territories into a modern federation. He appointed Mohamed bin Zayed as deputy crown prince of Abu Dhabi in November 2003, shortly before his death. His legacy includes establishing the foundations of the modern UAE state and ensuring the succession of his family's rule in Abu Dhabi.
achievements: Founded the United Arab Emirates in 1971
Served as first UAE president for 33 years
Transformed Abu Dhabi into modern emirate

figures.name: Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.nameAr: خليفة بن زايد بن سلطان آل نهيان
figures.title: Second President of the UAE and Ruler of Abu Dhabi
figures.roleDescription: Second president of the United Arab Emirates from 2004 to 2022
figures.significance: Served as the second president of the UAE and modernized the country into a regional economic powerhouse.
figures.biography: Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born on 7 September 1948 at Qasr Al-Muwaiji in Al Ain. He was the eldest son of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and became crown prince of Abu Dhabi on 1 February 1969. He succeeded his father as ruler of Abu Dhabi on 2 November 2004 and was elected president of the UAE the following day. During his presidency, the UAE became a regional economic powerhouse and strengthened ties with the United States and Israel. He established major cultural institutions including the Louvre Abu Dhabi and founded Etihad Airways. The world's tallest building, Burj Khalifa, was renamed in his honor in 2010. In January 2014, he suffered a stroke and his half-brother Mohamed bin Zayed took over day-to-day governance while Khalifa retained ceremonial powers until his death on 13 May 2022.
achievements: Led UAE's transformation into regional economic powerhouse
Established Louvre Abu Dhabi and major cultural centers
Founded Etihad Airways
Had world's tallest building named in his honor

figures.name: Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.nameAr: محمد بن زايد بن سلطان آل نهيان
figures.title: Third President of the UAE and Ruler of Abu Dhabi
figures.roleDescription: Current president of the United Arab Emirates since 2022
figures.significance: Current president of the UAE and one of the most powerful Arab leaders, known for authoritarian governance and regional influence.
figures.biography: Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born on 11 March 1961 at Oasis Hospital in Al Ain. He is the third son of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and graduated from the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in April 1979. He served in various military roles before becoming crown prince of Abu Dhabi in November 2004. After his brother Khalifa suffered a stroke in 2014, Mohamed became the de facto ruler of Abu Dhabi and president of the UAE. He officially became president on 14 May 2022 following Khalifa's death. Mohamed is known for his opposition to the Muslim Brotherhood and Iran, and has been involved in regional conflicts in Yemen, Libya, and Sudan. Under his leadership, the UAE signed the Abraham Accords with Israel in 2020. He controls sovereign wealth funds worth an estimated $1 trillion and was named by The New York Times as the most powerful Arab ruler in 2019.
achievements: Graduated from Royal Military Academy Sandhurst
Signed Abraham Accords normalizing relations with Israel
Controls sovereign wealth funds worth $1 trillion
Named most powerful Arab ruler by The New York Times

figures.name: Khaled bin Mohamed Al Nahyan
figures.nameAr: خالد بن محمد بن زايد آل نهيان
figures.title: Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi
figures.roleDescription: Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi and heir apparent
figures.significance: Current crown prince of Abu Dhabi and designated successor to his father Mohamed bin Zayed.
figures.biography: Sheikh Khaled bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born on 8 January 1982 and is the eldest son of Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He graduated from the University of Arizona in 2004 with a bachelor's in Political Science, followed by a master's degree from Georgetown University Walsh School of Foreign Service in 2007. He began his political career in security roles, being appointed head of national security on 15 February 2016 and deputy national security adviser on 16 January 2017. On 29 March 2023, he was appointed crown prince of Abu Dhabi by his father and chairman of the Abu Dhabi Executive Council. He holds key positions including membership on the boards of ADNOC and Abu Dhabi Investment Authority. He is married to Sheikha Fatima bint Suroor Al Nahyan and they have four children together.
achievements: Appointed Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi in 2023
Graduated from Georgetown University Walsh School of Foreign Service
Chairman of UAE Genomics Council
Board member of Abu Dhabi Investment Authority

figures.name: Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.nameAr: منصور بن زايد بن سلطان آل نهيان
figures.title: Vice President and Deputy Prime Minister of the UAE
figures.roleDescription: Vice President, Deputy Prime Minister, and Minister of Presidential Court
figures.significance: Senior UAE leader and owner of Manchester City Football Club, known for sports investments and sovereign wealth management.
figures.biography: Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan was born on 20 November 1970, the fifth son of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan. He graduated from the United Arab Emirates University with a bachelor's degree in international affairs in 1993. He has held various senior government positions including chairman of the presidential office (1997), first minister of presidential affairs, and deputy prime minister (2009). On 29 March 2023, he was appointed as the UAE's second vice president. He is chairman of Mubadala Investment Company and Emirates Investment Authority, controlling sovereign wealth funds worth hundreds of billions. Mansour is internationally known as the owner of Manchester City Football Club through his Abu Dhabi United Group, which he acquired in 2008. The club has won multiple Premier League titles and the Champions League under his ownership. He also owns stakes in various other sports clubs through City Football Group.
achievements: Owner of Manchester City Football Club
Chairman of Mubadala Investment Company
Vice President of the UAE
Led Manchester City to Champions League victory in 2023

figures.name: Theyab bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Chairman of Abu Dhabi Crown Prince's Court
figures.roleDescription: Senior royal and chairman of multiple government entities
figures.significance: Brother of Crown Prince Khaled and chairman of key Abu Dhabi institutions including Etihad Rail.
figures.biography: Sheikh Theyab bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan is the brother of Crown Prince Khaled bin Mohamed Al Nahyan and son of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He serves as chairman of the Abu Dhabi Crown Prince's Court and is a member of the Executive Council. He also holds the position of chairman of the Early Childhood Authority, Abu Dhabi Transport Company, and Etihad Rail, demonstrating his role in key infrastructure and development projects in Abu Dhabi. He is married to Sheikha Latifa bint Hamdan Al Nahyan and they have three children together: Fatima, Salama, and Zayed.
achievements: Chairman of Etihad Rail
Chairman of Abu Dhabi Crown Prince's Court
Member of Executive Council

figures.name: Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan
figures.title: First Lady of the UAE
figures.roleDescription: Wife of President Mohamed bin Zayed and prominent philanthropist
figures.significance: First Lady of the UAE known for her philanthropic work and arts patronage.
figures.biography: Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan is a member of the ruling Al Nahyan family and the wife of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. Her father, Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohammed Al Nahyan, was a former UAE deputy prime minister. She married Mohamed bin Zayed in 1980 and they have nine children together – four sons and five daughters – as well as two adopted daughters. She is known for her philanthropic activities and serves as founder and president of the Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan Foundation based in Abu Dhabi. She also chairs the Abu Dhabi Art Host Committee and holds senior positions including chair of the General Women's Union, president of the Supreme Council for Motherhood and Childhood, and supreme chair of the Family Development Foundation. She is a significant art collector, owning works by Pablo Picasso, Rudolf Ernst, and Damien Hirst.
achievements: Founder of Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan Foundation
Chair of General Women's Union
President of Supreme Council for Motherhood and Childhood
Significant art collector and patron

figures.name: Hamdan bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Senior Royal
figures.roleDescription: Son of President Mohamed bin Zayed
figures.significance: Son of the current UAE president and member of the next generation of Al Nahyan leadership.
figures.biography: Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born in 1988 and is one of the sons of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan. He married Sheikha Fakhra bint Khalifa Al Nahyan in 2021, connecting him to another branch of the Al Nahyan family. They have two sons together: Mohammed (born 15 August 2022) and Khalifa (born 28 February 2024). As a member of the next generation of the Al Nahyan family, he represents the future leadership of the emirate.

figures.name: Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Senior Royal and Intelligence Leader
figures.roleDescription: Brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed
figures.significance: Senior member of the Al Nahyan family involved in intelligence and security matters.
figures.biography: Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan is one of the brothers of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, being one of the 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi). He was mentioned as having competed for the position of Crown Prince before it was ultimately given to Khaled bin Mohamed by his father. Instead, Tahnoun was appointed to oversee a sovereign wealth fund worth $1.5 trillion, demonstrating his continued importance in the UAE's financial and economic affairs. He has been involved in various diplomatic and intelligence activities.
achievements: Oversees sovereign wealth fund worth $1.5 trillion

figures.name: Abdullah bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Senior Royal
figures.roleDescription: Brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed
figures.significance: One of the senior brothers in the Al Nahyan family and member of the Bani Fatima.
figures.biography: Sheikh Abdullah bin Zayed Al Nahyan is one of the brothers of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He is one of the 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi), making him a full brother to Mohamed, Hamdan, Hazza, Tahnoun, and Mansour. As a senior member of the ruling family, he plays an important role in the governance and administration of Abu Dhabi and the UAE.

figures.name: Hamdan bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Senior Royal
figures.roleDescription: Brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed
figures.significance: One of the senior brothers in the Al Nahyan family and member of the Bani Fatima.
figures.biography: Sheikh Hamdan bin Zayed Al Nahyan is one of the brothers of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He is one of the 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi), making him a full brother to Mohamed, Hazza, Tahnoun, Mansour, and Abdullah. As a senior member of the ruling family, he holds important positions in the governance structure of Abu Dhabi and the UAE.

figures.name: Hazza bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Senior Royal
figures.roleDescription: Brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed
figures.significance: One of the senior brothers in the Al Nahyan family and member of the Bani Fatima.
figures.biography: Sheikh Hazza bin Zayed Al Nahyan is one of the brothers of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He is one of the 'Bani Fatima' (sons of Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi), making him a full brother to Mohamed, Hamdan, Tahnoun, Mansour, and Abdullah. As a senior member of the ruling family, he plays a role in the governance and administration of the emirate and the federation.

figures.name: Sultan bin Khalifa Al Nahyan
figures.title: Senior Royal
figures.roleDescription: Son of former President Khalifa bin Zayed
figures.significance: Eldest son of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan.
figures.biography: Sheikh Sultan bin Khalifa Al Nahyan is the eldest son of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Sheikha Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei. He is married to Sheikha bint Saif Al Nahyan and they have three sons and three daughters together. As the son of a former president and member of the senior generation of the Al Nahyan family, he holds an important position within the royal family structure.

figures.name: Mohammed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan
figures.title: Senior Royal
figures.roleDescription: Son of former President Khalifa bin Zayed
figures.significance: Son of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan.
figures.biography: Sheikh Mohammed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan is one of the sons of the late President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Sheikha Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei. He is married to Al-Yazia bint Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan and they have three sons together. As a member of the Al Nahyan family and son of a former president, he represents the continuing influence of Khalifa's branch of the family.

figures.name: Zayed bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Royal Family Member
figures.roleDescription: Son of President Mohamed bin Zayed
figures.significance: Youngest son of the current UAE president, representing the next generation of Al Nahyan leadership.
figures.biography: Sheikh Zayed bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born on 24 October 1995 and is the youngest son of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan. He graduated from the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in December 2020, following in the military tradition of his family. Named after his grandfather, the founding father of the UAE, he represents the next generation of Al Nahyan leadership.
achievements: Graduated from Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in 2020

figures.name: Issa bin Zayed Al Nahyan
figures.title: Royal Family Member
figures.roleDescription: Brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed
figures.significance: Member of the Al Nahyan royal family who was subject to international controversy over torture allegations.
figures.biography: Sheikh Issa bin Zayed is a member of the Al Nahyan royal family and brother of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. In April 2009, he became the subject of international controversy when he was allegedly shown in video footage torturing a man. This incident brought negative attention to the Al Nahyan family and raised questions about accountability within the royal family structure.

figures.name: Lubna Khalid Al Qasimi
figures.nameAr: لبنى بنت خالد بن سلطان القاسمي
figures.title: Sheikha, Minister, University President
figures.roleDescription: First female minister in UAE, current President of Zayed University
figures.significance: She was the first woman to hold a ministerial post in the UAE and served in multiple high-ranking government positions.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Lubna bint Khalid bin Sultan Al Qasimi was born on 4 February 1962 and is an Emirati politician and member of the ruling family of Sharjah, niece to Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi. She graduated from California State University, Chico with a bachelor's degree in Computer Science and has an Executive MBA from the American University of Sharjah.

After returning to the UAE in 1981, she worked as a programmer for software company Datamation, then became Dubai branch manager for the General Information Authority responsible for automating the federal government. She later worked for seven years in the Dubai Ports Authority before entering ministerial roles. She served as Minister of State for Tolerance, Minister of State for International Cooperation, and Minister of Economic and Planning of the UAE, making history as the first woman to hold a ministerial post in the UAE.

Since 2014, she has served as President of Zayed University. She has received numerous honors including being named the 36th most powerful woman in the world by Forbes and receiving an honorary Dame Commander of The Most Excellent Order of the British Empire in 2013.
achievements: First woman to hold a ministerial post in the UAE
36th most powerful woman in the world by Forbes
Honorary Dame Commander of The Most Excellent Order of the British Empire (2013)
Lifetime Achievement Award at Asia HRD Congress 2012

figures.name: Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi
figures.title: Sheikh, Deputy Ruler of Sharjah
figures.roleDescription: Deputy Ruler of Sharjah and President of University of Sharjah
figures.significance: He serves as Deputy Ruler of Sharjah and holds numerous leadership positions across government and private sectors.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi is a member of the ruling family of the Emirate of Sharjah and currently serves as Deputy Ruler of Sharjah. He holds a Bachelor of Science degree in Business Administration from Arkansas State University and earned a Master of Science in Computer Information Systems from University of Detroit Mercy.

In December 2021, he was appointed President of the University of Sharjah by the ruler Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi. He serves as Deputy Chairman of The Executive Council, chairman of the Sharjah National Oil Corporation, chairman of Sharjah Media Council, and chairman for Sharjah Petroleum Council. In the private sector, he has chaired Basma Group since 2005, Tilal Properties LLC since 2014, and Arada since 2017, among other leadership positions.

He founded the online news website Sharjah 24 in 2015 and launched OMNES Media in 2017. He is married to his cousin Sheikha Bodour bint Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi, daughter of the ruler of Sharjah. He has received several awards including The Most Outstanding Media Personality Award from the League of Arab States in 2016.
achievements: The Most Outstanding Media Personality Award for 2016 from the League of Arab States
Elected as the Humanitarian Envoy for The Big Heart Foundation (2017)
Jawaher Medal of Honour (2023)
Golden Medal of Honor from the European Royal Academy (2025)

figures.name: Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi
figures.title: Sheikh, Ruler of Sharjah
figures.roleDescription: Current ruler of Sharjah, author, historian, and academic
figures.significance: He has ruled Sharjah since 1972 and is renowned for his historical publications, educational initiatives, and cultural preservation efforts.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi was born on 2 July 1939 and is the current 11th ruler of the Emirate of Sharjah since January 1972. He succeeded his brother Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi after his assassination on January 25, 1972. He studied Agricultural Engineering at Cairo University (1971), completed a PhD in history at the University of Exeter (1985), and another PhD in political geography at Durham University (1999).

He served as the UAE's first Minister of Education in December 1971 and has been president of both the American University of Sharjah and University of Sharjah since 1997. As an academic, he became a visiting professor at University of Exeter (1998) and professor of modern history of the Gulf at University of Sharjah (1999). He has authored at least 82 books by 2023, including historical, literary, and theatrical works.

He ruled continuously except for a seven-day period in June 1987 during a coup led by his elder brother Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Mohammed Al-Qasimi. He has made significant donations to the University of Exeter, described as 'the university's single most important supporter.' He established the 'Irada' drug addiction treatment center in Sharjah in May 2018 and celebrated his 50th anniversary as ruler in January 2022.
achievements: First Minister of Education of UAE (1971)
Authored at least 82 books by 2023
Grand Cross with Collar of the Order of Zayed
Commander of the Legion of Honour (France)
Great Cross with Star of the Order of Merit of the Federal Republic of Germany

figures.name: Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi
figures.title: Sheikh, 9th Ruler of Sharjah
figures.roleDescription: Ruler of Sharjah and founding father of the UAE
figures.significance: He was a founding father of the United Arab Emirates and the 9th ruler of Sharjah until his assassination in 1972.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi was born in Sharjah in 1931 and served as the 9th ruler of the Emirate of Sharjah from 1965 until his murder in 1972. He became ruler following the exile of his Arab nationalist cousin Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi, who was removed with unanimous consent of the ruling family under British pressure. His status as ruler was confirmed by William Luce, the British Political Resident of the Persian Gulf, on 25 June 1965.

As a quiet and unassuming man, Khalid established Sharjah's first formal police force and played a key role in the negotiations that led to the foundation of the United Arab Emirates on 2 December 1971, making him a founding father of the nation. He was responsible for the demolition of Sharjah Fort in an attempt to erase the memory of his predecessor Saqr, though his brother Sultan convinced him to stop the demolition partway through.

On 24 January 1972, Saqr returned from exile in Egypt with mercenaries and seized power in a coup attempt, invading the Ruler's palace at approximately 2:30 PM. Despite being besieged by the Union Defence Force and Saqr's eventual surrender to UAE Minister of Defence Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Sheikh Khalid was killed in the action on 25 January 1972. He was succeeded by his brother Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.
achievements: Founding father of the United Arab Emirates
Established Sharjah's first formal police force

figures.name: Khalid bin Saqr Al Qasimi
figures.title: Sheikh, Former Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah
figures.roleDescription: Former Crown Prince and Deputy Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah
figures.significance: He served as Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah for 45 years before being removed in 2003 due to political differences.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Khalid bin Saqr Al Qasimi was born in 1940 and is the eldest son of Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi, who ruled Ras Al Khaimah from 1948 until 2010. He was named Crown Prince and Deputy Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah in 1958 at age 18 and served in this capacity for 45 years until his removal in June 2003. He completed his elementary schooling in Ras Al Khaimah, secondary education in Cairo, and higher education at Loughborough University in England.

As Crown Prince, he oversaw day-to-day government operations and served as principal contact with foreign governments, leading diplomatic missions to the United States during the Nixon, Reagan and Clinton Administrations. In 1999, President Clinton personally received him in the White House. He served as Chairman of the Board of several major institutions including the National Bank of Ras Al Khaimah, RAK National Oil Company, and RAK Cement Company, promoting economic development including airport and port expansion.

After 37 years in office, he was removed as Crown Prince on June 14, 2003, by his father and replaced by his younger half-brother Sheikh Saud bin Saqr al Qasimi. His removal caused street protests with thousands of demonstrators, requiring Abu Dhabi to send armored vehicles to restore order. Media reports attributed his removal to his opposition to the Iraq War and his vocal hostility to US intervention, which conflicted with UAE federal policy of strengthening cooperation with the US.
achievements: Served as Crown Prince for 45 years (1958-2003)
Led high-level diplomatic missions to the United States
Received by President Clinton in the White House (1999)
Promoted economic development including expansion of International Airport and Saqr Port

figures.name: Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi
figures.title: Sheikh, Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah
figures.roleDescription: Current ruler of Ras Al Khaimah
figures.significance: He has been the ruler of Ras Al Khaimah since 2010 and has overseen significant economic development and educational initiatives.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi was born in Dubai on 10 February 1956 and is the fourth son of Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi. He completed his primary and secondary education in Ras Al Khaimah, then attended the American University of Beirut in July 1973 to study economics. When the Lebanese civil war broke out in 1975, he transferred to the University of Michigan, where he received a bachelor's degree in economics and political science.

Returning to Ras Al Khaimah in 1979, he was appointed Chief of the Ruler's Court to assist his father in administration. He became Chairman of the Ras Al Khaimah Municipal Council in 1986 and in 1989 recruited Swiss-Lebanese engineer Khater Massaad to develop industrialization plans, most importantly establishing RAK Ceramics in 1991, which became the world's largest ceramics producer. He became Crown Prince and Deputy Ruler on 14 June 2003 when his father dismissed his elder half-brother Khalid for political differences.

On 27 October 2010, upon his father's death after 62 years of rule, Saud officially became Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah. Under his leadership, the emirate's GDP per capita improved from AED 35,000 in 2001 to AED 104,680 in 2018. He established numerous educational institutions including the American University of Ras Al Khaimah and RAK Medical and Health Sciences University, and created the Al Qasimi Foundation for Policy Research in 2009.
achievements: Co-founded RAK Ceramics, which became world's largest ceramics producer
Improved emirate GDP per capita from AED 35,000 to AED 104,680
Established American University of Ras Al Khaimah
Honorary doctorate from University of Bolton (2010)
Named Visionary Leader of the Year at Arabian Business Awards (2018)

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.title: Founder and Religious Reformer
figures.roleDescription: 18th century founder of the Wahhabi sect and patriarch of the Al ash-Sheikh family
figures.significance: Founded the Wahhabi sect of Islam and established the power-sharing pact with the Al Saud family that continues to this day.
figures.biography: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab was born in 1703 in the Nejd and became the founder of the Wahhabi sect which is today dominant in Saudi Arabia. He became influenced by the teachings of Ibn Taymiya, a medieval jurist of the Hanbali school of jurisprudence, and began to preach a simple, puritanical form of Islam that warned against religious innovations. In the 1740s, he moved to Diriyah in Nejd, where Muhammad ibn Saud was the local ruler. The two concluded a formal agreement in 1744 that became a mutual support pact between their families. Muhammad ibn Saud declared support for Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's religious cause, while Ibn Abd al-Wahhab supported the Al Saud's political authority. This combination of religious zeal and political leadership initiated a period of conquest that brought most of central Arabia and the Hejaz under Al Saud rule in what became known as the 'First Saudi State'. After his death in 1791, Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's legacy was carried on by his many descendants, who continued to hold positions of religious authority.
achievements: Founded the Wahhabi sect of Islam
Established the 1744 pact with Muhammad ibn Saud
Created the ideological foundation for Saudi expansion

figures.name: Abd Allah ibn Muhammad Abd al-Wahhab
figures.nameAr: عبد الله بن محمد عبد الوهاب
figures.title: Head of Saudi Religious Establishment
figures.roleDescription: Son of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and leader of religious establishment after his father's death
figures.significance: Became leader of the Saudi religious establishment after Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's death and endorsed further Al Saud expansion.
figures.biography: Abd Allah ibn Muhammad Abd al-Wahhab was the son of the founder Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and became leader of the Saudi religious establishment following his father's death in 1791. He endorsed further Al Saud expansion and wrote a number of tracts against Shia belief, continuing his father's religious mission. His leadership came during a critical period when the Saudi state was expanding but also facing growing Ottoman opposition. The Ottoman forces from Egypt eventually destroyed the Al Saud state in 1818, which was also a disaster for the Al ash-Sheikh family, though Abd Allah had died by this time.
achievements: Led Saudi religious establishment after Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
Wrote tracts against Shia belief
Endorsed Al Saud territorial expansion

figures.name: Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Latif Al ash-Sheikh
figures.nameAr: عبد الله بن عبد اللطيف آل الشيخ
figures.title: Head of Saudi Religious Establishment
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader during the early years of Abdul Aziz Al Saud's rule
figures.significance: Led the religious establishment during the transition period and strengthened ties with the returning Al Saud through marriage alliance.
figures.biography: Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Latif Al ash-Sheikh was a descendant of Ibn Abd al-Wahhab who led the ulema during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. When the Al Saud were defeated by the Al Rashid in 1891 and went into exile, he decided to side with the Al-Rashid and moved to Ha'il, indicating that preservation of the Wahhabi cause took precedence over the family compact with the Al Saud. However, when Abdul Aziz Al Saud returned from exile in 1902 and re-established the Saudi state around Riyadh, Abd Allah changed sides again and re-joined the Al Saud. Abdul Aziz accepted this change of heart and cemented the relationship with the Al ash-Sheikh through matrimonial links, including marrying Abd Allah's daughter. He was the father of Tarfa bint Abdullah, who became one of Abdul Aziz's wives and mother of King Faisal.
achievements: Led religious establishment during Al Saud restoration
Strengthened Al ash-Sheikh alliance through marriage ties
Father of Tarfa bint Abdullah, mother of King Faisal

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh
figures.title: Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: First Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia from 1953 to 1969
figures.significance: Served as the first Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia and was father of Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh.
figures.biography: Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh served as Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia from 1953 to 1969, making him the first person to hold this restored religious office in the modern Kingdom. He was a prominent religious scholar who played a significant role in establishing the formal religious institutions of Saudi Arabia. His influence extended beyond his official position, as he was respected throughout the Islamic world for his scholarship. He was the father of Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh, who would later serve as Minister of Justice and Chairman of the Majlis ash-Shura. The office of Grand Mufti was abolished by King Faisal in 1969, the same year Muhammad ibn Ibrahim died, but was later restored in 1993.
achievements: First Grand Mufti of modern Saudi Arabia (1953-1969)
Established formal religious institutions
Father of future government minister Abdullah ibn Muhammad

figures.name: Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh
figures.nameAr: عبد الله بن محمد آل الشيخ
figures.title: Chairman of Majlis ash-Shura
figures.roleDescription: Minister of Justice (1992-2009) and Chairman of Consultative Assembly since 2009
figures.significance: Served as Saudi Minister of Justice for 17 years and implemented major judicial reforms including the creation of specialized courts.
figures.biography: Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh was born in Diriyah in 1948 and is a member of the noted Al ash-Sheikh family of religious scholars. He was educated by his father, Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh, the former Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia. He graduated with a bachelor's degree in Sharia from the Shariah College in Riyadh in 1975, then studied at Al-Azhar University in Cairo before returning to earn his PhD in Fiqh from Imam Mohammed bin Saudi University in 1987. After obtaining his doctorate, he became a lecturer and professor until his appointment as Minister of Justice in November 1992, replacing Mohammed bin Jubair. During his tenure, he drew up the judiciary law of 2007 on the King's instructions, creating specialized courts and a Supreme Court. He was replaced as justice minister in February 2009 in a cabinet reshuffle aimed at replacing conservative incumbents with more progressive candidates, and was appointed Chairman of the Majlis ash-Shura the same year.
achievements: Saudi Minister of Justice (1992-2009)
Created judiciary law of 2007 establishing specialized courts
Chairman of Majlis ash-Shura since 2009

figures.name: Abdulaziz ibn Abdullah Al ash-Sheikh
figures.nameAr: عبد العزيز بن عبد الله آل الشيخ
figures.title: Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Third Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia from 1999 to 2025
figures.significance: Served as Grand Mufti for 26 years and was head of the Council of Senior Religious Scholars until his death in 2025.
figures.biography: Abdulaziz ibn Abdullah Al ash-Sheikh was born in Mecca on November 30, 1943, and became the third Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia. He lost his father when he was eight years old in 1951, and lost his vision at age 20 in 1963. In 1969-70, he assumed leadership at the Sheikh Muhammad bin Ibrahim Mosque in Dukhna, Riyadh, and in 1979 was appointed assistant professor at the College of Sharia in Mecca. He became Deputy Grand Mufti in 1995, and in June 1999, King Fahd appointed him as Grand Mufti following the death of Abdulaziz bin Baz. As Grand Mufti, he was head of the Council of Senior Religious Scholars and its sub-committee, the Permanent Committee for Islamic Research and Issuing Fatwas. He issued numerous controversial fatwas during his tenure and died in Riyadh on September 23, 2025, at age 81.
achievements: Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia (1999-2025)
Head of Council of Senior Religious Scholars
Issued fatwas on major religious and social issues

figures.name: Saleh bin Abdul-Aziz Al ash-Sheikh
figures.nameAr: صالح بن عبد العزيز آل الشيخ
figures.title: Minister of Islamic Affairs
figures.roleDescription: Saudi Minister of Islamic Affairs, Endowments, Call and Guidance
figures.significance: Has served as Saudi Minister of Islamic Affairs since 1996 and is known for his religious writings and international Islamic diplomacy.
figures.biography: Saleh bin Abdul-Aziz Al ash-Sheikh was born in 1959 and is a member of the influential Al ash-Sheikh religious family. He is well known for his studies of the fatwas of his grandfather, Sheikh Muhammad bin Ibrahim, and is a graduate from Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University in Riyadh from the Faculty of Fundamentals of Islamic Religion. He has served as Minister of Islamic Affairs, Endowments, Call and Guidance since 2000, with a brief interruption from December 2014 to February 2015. He is upon the Hanbali school of thought in Islam and has written extensively on Islamic faith and practice, including works like 'This is Islam' and 'Islamic Principles for the Muslim's Attitude During Fitan'. He has engaged in international Islamic diplomacy, meeting with ministers from Bosnia, Pakistan, and other countries to discuss religious cooperation and has been involved in regional religious disputes, including calling upon Qatar to change the name of a mosque in Doha.
achievements: Minister of Islamic Affairs since 1996
Author of multiple Islamic religious texts
International Islamic diplomatic engagement

figures.name: Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh
figures.nameAr: طرفة بنت عبد الله آل الشيخ
figures.title: Queen Consort
figures.roleDescription: Wife of King Abdulaziz and mother of King Faisal
figures.significance: Mother of King Faisal of Saudi Arabia and daughter of prominent religious leader Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Latif.
figures.biography: Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh was born in 1884, the daughter of Abdullah bin Abdullatif Al Sheikh, a principal religious teacher and adviser to the Emir of Nejd. She was one of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's eighth generation direct descendants. Tarfa married Emir Abdulaziz in 1902 immediately after he captured Riyadh, becoming his third wife. This marriage was a strategic move to strengthen the links between the Al Sauds and the Al Sheikhs. Her sisters also married into the Al Saud family - Munira married Abdulaziz's half-brother Muhammad bin Abdul Rahman, and Sara married his full-brother Saad bin Abdul Rahman. Tarfa and Abdulaziz's first child, Noura, was born in 1904, followed by their son Faisal in April 1906. Tragically, Tarfa died in October 1906 when Faisal was just six months old. Her son Faisal would later become King of Saudi Arabia in 1964.
achievements: Mother of King Faisal of Saudi Arabia
Strengthened Al Saud-Al ash-Sheikh alliance through marriage
Eighth generation descendant of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab

figures.name: Mohammed bin Hassan Al Khater
figures.title: Head of Al Khater in Bahrain
figures.roleDescription: Tribal leader and benefactor
figures.significance: He helped Abdul Rahman ibn Faisal (father of King Abdul Aziz) when he sought refuge in Bahrain, offering his house and facilitating his meeting with the Al Khalifa rulers.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Mohammed bin Hassan Al Khater was a prominent member of the Al Khater family and served as the head of Al Khater in Bahrain. He played a crucial role in Saudi-Bahraini relations when he found Abdul Rahman ibn Faisal, the last ruler of the Second Saudi State, seeking refuge in his mosque in Bahrain after being defeated by Al Rashid and forced into exile. Mohammed bin Hassan offered him and his companions, including his son the future King Abdul Aziz, his house to sleep in and helped Abdul Rahman reach Isa ibn Ali Al-Khalifa.

Sheikh Mohammed bin Hassan had solid ties with the heads of Al Khalifa, the ruling family of Bahrain, based on respect and good manners. He owned many farms in Bahrain, including one near Bahrain International Airport that remains visible today. He was instrumental in prisoner exchanges, helping to trade Sheikh Ibrahim bin Ali Al Khalifa for Sheikh Qassim bin Mohammed Al-Thani, where Sheikh Ibrahim was captured in Qatar and Sheikh Qassim was captured in Bahrain. Later, family ties were strengthened when he married Sheikha Sabika, daughter of Sheikh Qassim.
achievements: Provided refuge and assistance to Abdul Rahman ibn Faisal during his exile
Facilitated important prisoner exchange between Al Khalifa and Al Thani
Established strong diplomatic relations between Al Khater and ruling families

figures.name: Mohammed bin Khamis
figures.title: Chief of Al Bidda
figures.roleDescription: Tribal chief and successor to Buhur bin Jubrun
figures.significance: He was a prominent member of the Al-Buainain tribe and successor of Buhur bin Jubrun as chief of Al Bidda, though he was embroiled in controversy for murdering a native of Bahrain.
figures.biography:
Mohammed bin Khamis was a prominent member of the Al-Buainain tribe who succeeded Buhur bin Jubrun as chief of Al Bidda in 1828. His leadership was marked by controversy when he murdered a native of Bahrain, which prompted the Al Khalifa sheikh to imprison him. This incident had significant consequences for the Al-Buainain tribe and the region.

In response to Mohammed bin Khamis's imprisonment, the Al-Buainain tribe revolted against the Al Khalifa. This rebellion provoked a harsh response from the Al Khalifa, who destroyed the tribe's fort and evicted them to Fuwayrit and Ar Ru'ays. This incident allowed the Al Khalifa to gain additional jurisdiction over the town of Al Bidda. With essentially no effective ruler after these events, Al Bidda and the nearby settlement of Doha became a sanctuary for pirates and outlaws, marking a turbulent period in the region's history.

figures.name: Buhur bin Jubrun
figures.title: Ruler and Initial Founder of Doha
figures.roleDescription: Chief of the Al-Buainain tribe and founder
figures.significance: He was the ruler and initial founder of Doha, and chief of the Al-Buainain tribe when the British political resident John MacLeod visited Al Bidda in January 1823.
figures.biography:
Buhur bin Jubrun was the ruler and initial founder of Doha, serving as the chief of the Al-Buainain tribe in the early 19th century. In January 1823, the British political resident John MacLeod visited Al Bidda specifically to meet with Buhur bin Jubrun. During this significant diplomatic encounter, MacLeod noted that Al Bidda was the only substantial trading port in the peninsula at that time, highlighting the importance of the settlement under Buhur bin Jubrun's leadership.

As the founder of Doha, Buhur bin Jubrun played a crucial role in establishing what would become Qatar's capital city. His leadership of the Al-Buainain tribe and control over the important trading port of Al Bidda made him a key figure in the region's early commercial and political development. His position as both tribal chief and town ruler represented the traditional Arabian leadership structure of the time, where tribal authority and territorial control were closely interlinked.
achievements: Founded the city of Doha
Established Al Bidda as the peninsula's main trading port
Conducted diplomatic relations with British officials

figures.name: Theyab bin Saqer Al Noaimi
figures.title: Lieutenant General, Chief of Staff of Bahrain Defence Force
figures.roleDescription: Current Chief of Staff of the Bahrain Defence Force
figures.significance: He serves as the Chief of Staff of the Bahrain Defence Force, holding the rank of Lieutenant General.
figures.biography:
Lieutenant General Theyab bin Saqer Al Noaimi currently serves as the Chief of Staff of the Bahrain Defence Force (BDF). He holds this senior military position in the BDF, which was established in 1968 and consists of the Royal Bahraini Air Force, Royal Bahraini Army, Royal Bahraini Navy and the Royal Guard.

The BDF numbers about 18,000 personnel and is under the direct command and leadership of a commander-in-chief who holds the rank of field marshal. Al Noaimi's role as Chief of Staff makes him one of the most senior military officers in the Bahraini armed forces, working under the overall command structure that includes Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad Al Khalifa as Deputy Supreme Commander and Field Marshal Khalifa bin Ahmed Al Khalifa as Commander-in-Chief.

figures.name: Essa Abdulla Al Basha Al Noaimi
figures.title: Distinguished figure
figures.roleDescription: Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles
figures.significance: He received Sudan's second-highest honour, the Order of the Two Niles (Class I: Grand Cordon) in 2009.
figures.biography:
Essa Abdulla Al Basha Al Noaimi is a distinguished figure who was honored by Sudan with the Order of the Two Niles in 2009. He received the First Class Grand Cordon of this prestigious order, which is Sudan's second-highest honour after the Order of the Republic.

The Order of the Two Niles was established on 16 November 1961 during Ibrahim Abboud's military government and is given to both Sudanese and foreign nationals, civilians and military personnel, who have provided great services to the state. Al Noaimi's receipt of this high honor in 2009 indicates his significant contributions, though the specific nature of his services is not detailed in the available records.
achievements: Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles (Grand Cordon, 2009)

figures.name: Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Former Foreign Minister of the United Arab Emirates
figures.roleDescription: Former UAE Foreign Minister and member of Ajman's ruling family
figures.significance: He served as the UAE's foreign minister and is a member of the ruling Al Nuaimi family of Ajman emirate.
figures.biography:
Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi is a member of the ruling family of Ajman, Al Nuaimi, and served as the former foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates. He holds a bachelor's degree in petroleum engineering, which he received from the University of Cairo in 1967.

Nuaimi started his career at the department of oil and industrial affairs in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi before joining the Emirati ministry of foreign affairs. Until 1975, he worked in various capacities at the foreign ministry. In 1975, he was named director of political affairs department at the foreign ministry, and in 1976 became undersecretary for foreign affairs. He served as minister of state for foreign affairs from 1977 to 1990, when Hamdan bin Zayed succeeded him in the post. In recognition of his diplomatic service, he received the Order of the Two Niles from Sudan in 2018 and the Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan in 2024.
achievements: Former UAE Foreign Minister
Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles (2018)
Recipient of Japan's Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun (2024)

figures.name: Ali Rashid Al Noaimi
figures.title: Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University
figures.roleDescription: Former Al Islah member turned UAE University administrator
figures.significance: He is a former member of the banned Islamist group Al Islah who became Vice Chancellor of UAE University.
figures.biography:
Ali Rashid Al Noaimi serves as the Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University and is a former member of Al Islah, the UAE-based Islamist political group affiliated with the Muslim Brotherhood. Al Noaimi's experience with Al Islah provides insight into the organization's operations and ideology.

As a former Al Islah member who later took a senior academic position, Al Noaimi has been critical of the organization's loyalties. He stated that Al Islah members 'get their orders from outside' and 'they are not loyal to their country,' providing testimony about the group's alleged foreign connections and influence. His transition from involvement with the banned political organization to becoming a senior university administrator reflects the complex political and social dynamics within the UAE's educated elite.
achievements: Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University

figures.name: Saad Mohammed Al-Rumaihi
figures.title: Sports Journalist and Club Co-founder
figures.roleDescription: Sports journalist at Al Raya newspaper and co-founder of Al-Gharafa Sports Club
figures.significance: Co-founded Al-Gharafa Sports Club and suggested the initial idea for its establishment while working as a sports journalist.
figures.biography:
Saad Mohammed Al-Rumaihi was a sports journalist working at the Al Raya newspaper in Qatar during the late 1970s. He was one of the founding members of Al-Gharafa Sports Club, established on June 6, 1979, alongside prominent members of the Al Thani royal family including Sheikh Mohammed bin Jassim Al Thani, Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim Al Thani, Sheikh Hamad bin Faisal Al Thani, and Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Thani.

According to a documentary produced by Al Kass sports channel about the history of Al-Gharafa Sports Club, Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim mentioned that the idea to establish the club was initially suggested by Saad Al-Rumaihi during his time as a sports journalist. His suggestion led to the creation of what would become one of Qatar's most successful football clubs, which has won multiple league championships and domestic cups. The club was founded with the intent of creating sports facilities for the youth in the Al Gharrafa region of Al Rayyan municipality.
achievements: Co-founded Al-Gharafa Sports Club in 1979
Worked as sports journalist at Al Raya newspaper
Suggested the initial idea for establishing Al-Gharafa Sports Club

figures.name: Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa
figures.title: Poet, Writer, and Political Activist
figures.roleDescription: Former BDF officer, poet, writer, and first political prisoner of the 2011 Bahraini uprising
figures.significance: Became the first political prisoner in the Bahraini uprising after delivering a significant speech at Pearl Roundabout calling for unity between Sunnis and Shias.
figures.biography:
Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa was born on January 1, 1977, and belongs to the Albuflasa Bedouin clan in Bahrain. He is a poet, writer, and former independent candidate for the Bahraini Parliament in the 2010 Parliamentary elections, as well as a member of the Bahraini youth parliament. Formerly a Bahrain Defence Force officer, he later became employed at the court of Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa.

During the 2010 Parliamentary Elections, Albuflasa stood as an independent candidate calling for change, publicly criticizing the Bahraini parliament's neglect of its monitoring and legislative role. He subsequently withdrew in favor of the Al-Menbar Islamic Society's candidate Mohamed Al-Emadi. On February 15, 2011, the second day of Bahrain Arab Spring protests, Albuflasa addressed the crowd at Pearl Roundabout in what was described as a balanced speech. Wearing a traditional Arab thawb, he pointed to the need for genuine political reform and expressed support for protesters' demands, including calls for an end to sectarian discrimination against the Shia majority. His speech was particularly noteworthy because he was a religiously conservative Sunni while most protesters were Shia or secular Sunnis.

After leaving the assembly that evening, Albuflasa disappeared and became the first political prisoner of the Bahraini uprising. Three weeks later, the government acknowledged his arrest by security forces. He was reportedly subjected to mistreatment, humiliation, and torture during detention, including solitary confinement in a 1-meter by 1-meter cell. He was tried by a military court in secret on February 27 and sentenced to two months in prison in mid-March. Despite completing his sentence by April 15, his detention was extended for 45 days, leading him to begin a hunger strike on June 1. He was finally released on July 24, 2011, and was greeted by hundreds of Sunni and Shia supporters chanting 'Brothers Sunna and Shia,' playing an important role in reducing sectarian conflict and serving as a symbol for unity and tolerance.
achievements: First political prisoner of the 2011 Bahraini uprising
Delivered significant speech promoting Sunni-Shia unity at Pearl Roundabout
Served as symbol for unity and tolerance during sectarian tensions
Former Bahrain Defence Force officer and parliamentary candidate

figures.name: Rashed Albuflasa
figures.title: Family Member
figures.roleDescription: Brother of Mohamed Albuflasa and family spokesperson
figures.significance: Served as family spokesperson during Mohamed Albuflasa's imprisonment and explained the motivation behind his brother's political activism.
figures.biography:
Rashed Albuflasa is the brother of Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa and served as a family spokesperson during the 2011 Bahraini uprising. When his brother Mohamed was arrested and became the first political prisoner of the uprising, Rashed explained to the media the significance and motivation behind Mohamed's speech at Pearl Roundabout.

According to Rashed, Mohamed's speech was intended to calm the flames of anger caused by efforts to create disagreement between Shiites and Sunnis. He wanted to show there was no difference between them and that both were Bahraini. Rashed clarified that while the government was surprised and regarded the speech as an attack on them, Mohamed was not against the royal family and government, but rather sought to promote national unity and bridge sectarian divides during a time of significant political tension in Bahrain.

figures.name: Sabah I bin Jaber
figures.nameAr: أبو سلمان صباح بن جابر الصباح الأول
figures.title: First Ruler of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Founder and first ruler of the Sheikhdom of Kuwait
figures.significance: Founded the Al-Sabah dynasty and established Kuwait as an independent sheikhdom in 1752.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abu Salman Sabah I bin Jaber Al Sabah (c. 1700–1762) was chosen by his community as the first ruler of Kuwait. The families of the Bani Utbah arrived in Kuwait in the mid-1700s and settled after receiving permission from the Emir of al-Hasa Sa'dun bin Muhammad. In 1718, the head of each family in the town of Kuwait gathered and chose Sabah I bin Jaber as the Sheikh of Kuwait, becoming a governor of sorts underneath the Emir of Al Hasa.

In 1752, Kuwait became independent after an agreement between Sabah and the Emir of Al Hasa in which Al Hasa recognised Sabah I bin Jaber's independent rule over Kuwait. In exchange, Kuwait would not ally itself with or support the enemies of Al Hasa or interfere in the internal affairs of Al Hasa. Upon his death in 1762, Sabah I was succeeded by his youngest son, Abdullah bin Sabah, establishing the hereditary rule of the Al-Sabah family.
achievements: Founded the Sheikhdom of Kuwait
Established Kuwait's independence in 1752
Founded the Al-Sabah ruling dynasty

figures.name: Abdullah I Al-Sabah
figures.title: Second Ruler of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Second ruler of the Sheikhdom of Kuwait
figures.significance: Ruled Kuwait for 52 years from 1762 to 1814, consolidating the Al-Sabah dynasty's power.
figures.biography:
Abdullah I bin Sabah Al-Sabah (1740 – 3 May 1814) was the second ruler of the Sheikhdom of Kuwait, ruling from 1762 to 1814. He was the youngest son of Sabah bin Jaber, the founder of the Al-Sabah dynasty, and succeeded his father upon the latter's death in 1762.

Abdullah I ruled for over five decades, one of the longest reigns in Kuwaiti history. During his long reign, he consolidated the power and influence of the Al-Sabah family and helped establish Kuwait as a significant trading hub in the Persian Gulf region. He was succeeded by his son Jaber I Al-Sabah, continuing the hereditary succession that remains in place today.
achievements: Ruled Kuwait for 52 years
Consolidated Al-Sabah dynasty power
Established Kuwait as a trading hub

figures.name: Mubarak Al-Sabah
figures.title: Mubarak the Great
figures.roleDescription: Seventh ruler of Kuwait who forged alliance with Britain
figures.significance: Forged the critical alliance between Kuwait and Britain and seized power through the assassination of his brother in 1896.
figures.biography:
The reign of Mubarak the Great (1896–1915) forged the critical alliance between Kuwait and Britain. In the late 19th century, resurgent Ottoman power coupled with rising Al-Saud power drew Kuwait closer to the Ottomans. When Sheikh Mohammed came to power in 1892, disagreements soon arose between him and his brother Mubarak, with Muhammad dealing with this by busying Mubarak with affairs outside the capital.

In 1896, Mubarak summoned his sons, Jabir and Salim, and some supporters and rode to Kuwait, secretly entering Muhammed's house where he killed Muhammed and his brother Jarrah. In the morning, Mubarak announced that his brothers had died, and that he ruled in their stead. His reign marked a turning point in Kuwait's relationship with the Ottoman Empire and established the foundation for Kuwait's later independence under British protection.
achievements: Forged Kuwait-Britain alliance
Seized power and established his rule in 1896
Ruled Kuwait from 1896 to 1915

figures.name: Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah
figures.title: Tenth Ruler of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Tenth ruler who founded Kuwait's military and was father to multiple future emirs
figures.significance: Founded the military of Kuwait and was the father of four future emirs including Jaber, Sabah, Nawaf, and Mishal.
figures.biography:
Damat Sheikh Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (1885 – 29 January 1950) was the tenth ruler of the Sheikhdom of Kuwait from 29 March 1921 until his death. Ahmad was the son of Jaber II Al-Sabah and succeeded his uncle Salem Al-Sabah in February 1921. He was the lead cavalry commander, founder of the military of Kuwait and the Directorate of Public Security Force.

During his reign, Ahmad faced various challenges including requests for financial aid from Palestinian authorities in 1936, which he refused due to treaty relations with Britain. He served as minister of finance from 1940 to 1950. Ahmad was married several times and had many notable children who would later play crucial roles in Kuwait's history. His sons included four future emirs: Jaber Al-Ahmad (ruled 1977–2006), Sabah Al-Ahmad (ruled 2006–2020), Nawaf Al-Ahmad (ruled 2020–2023), and Mishal Al-Ahmad (current emir since 2023). Ahmad died in 1950 at Dasman Palace in Kuwait.
achievements: Founded the military of Kuwait
Established the Directorate of Public Security Force
Father of four future emirs of Kuwait

figures.name: Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah
figures.title: First Emir of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Ended British protectorate and established Kuwait's independence and constitution
figures.significance: Ended the British protectorate status of Kuwait in 1961 and introduced Kuwait's Constitution in 1962.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah was the eleventh ruler and first Emir of Kuwait, ruling from 1950 to 1965. He achieved Kuwait's most significant milestone by ending the British protectorate status of Kuwait through signing a treaty with the British on 19 June 1961, making Kuwait a fully independent nation.

Following independence, Sheikh Abdullah established the foundations of modern Kuwait by introducing the Constitution of Kuwait in 1962, followed by the establishment of the Parliament in 1963. These democratic institutions transformed Kuwait from a traditional sheikhdom into a constitutional monarchy with parliamentary representation. His reign marked the transition of Kuwait into the modern era and established the governmental framework that continues to operate today.
achievements: Achieved Kuwait's independence from Britain in 1961
Introduced Kuwait's Constitution in 1962
Established Kuwait's Parliament in 1963

figures.name: Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah
figures.title: Current Emir of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Current ruler of Kuwait and former head of intelligence services
