On 15 September 2020, Zayani signed the official Bahrain–Israel normalization agreement at the White House in Washington, D.C. In May 2023, he made history by becoming the first Bahraini foreign minister to visit Ukraine since diplomatic relations were established in 1992.
achievements: First GCC Secretary General with military background
Signed Bahrain-Israel normalization agreement
First Bahraini Foreign Minister to visit Ukraine

figures.name: Khalil Al-Zayani
figures.title: Football Player and Coach
figures.roleDescription: Former Al-Ettifaq player and coach, Saudi national team player
figures.significance: Most prolific Al-Ettifaq player who became the first Saudi Arabian manager to win the league title and coached both club and national team.
figures.biography:
Khalil Al-Zayani was signed by Al-Ettifaq in 1962 as a wingback/centerback defender at just 15 years old, graduating from the youth team Academy. The young Zayani had to balance his education with his professional football career while playing for Al-Ettifaq. He was recognized for his vision and charisma on the field, leading to his appointment as squad captain at barely 16 years old in the 1964-1965 season. During his playing career, he helped Al-Ettifaq win their first Crown Prince Cup in 1964-1965 and their first King Cup, reaching 5 finals in different competitions and winning 2 trophies for his club.

Zayani played his entire club career for Al-Ettifaq and was also selected for the Saudi National Team. He retired from club football in 1971 and from the national team in 1973 at the age of just 26. After his playing career, he returned to Al-Ettifaq as a coach, leading the club to historic success including their first Saudi Premier League title in 1982-83, becoming the first Saudi team to win without any defeats. Under his management, Al-Ettifaq also won the 1986-87 Saudi Premier League, the 1988 Arab Club Champions Cup, and the 1988 Gulf Club Champions Cup. He temporarily left the club in March 1984 to manage the Saudi national team, replacing Mario Zagallo during the 7th Arabian Gulf Cup tournament.
achievements: First Saudi Arabian manager to win the league title
Led Al-Ettifaq to undefeated league championship
Managed both Al-Ettifaq and Saudi national team

figures.name: Afnan Rashid Al Zayani
figures.title: Chef and Businesswoman
figures.roleDescription: First cookbook author in Bahrain and pioneering TV chef
figures.significance: First person to publish a cookbook in Bahrain and hosted the country's first cooking show, becoming a prominent advocate for women's economic empowerment.
figures.biography:
Afnan Rashid Al Zayani was born in Manama, where she grew up in a close-knit, traditional family. She received her Bachelor of Arts in Food Processing Technology from Texas Tech University in 1976. Al Zayani became the first person to publish a cookbook in Bahrain in 1983 and made her TV debut in 1986 with the show السفرة الخليجية ('Gulf Dining'), becoming the host of the country's first cooking show. In 1988, she published 'A Taste of the Arabian Gulf', the first Gulf cuisine cookbook published in English.

Beyond her culinary career, Al Zayani pursued leadership in the family business and assumed positions that culminated in her buying one of the family companies in 1999. She chaired the Bahrain Businesswomen's Association from 2002 to 2004 and founded the Middle East and North Africa Businesswomen's Network in 2006. She was named the Middle East's leading businesswoman in 2004 by Hayatouki magazine and was recognized by Forbes Middle East as one of the '50 Most Powerful Women in the Middle East' in 2005. In 2009, she won the 'Best Arabic Cookbook' prize at the Gourmand World Cookbook Awards for her book Afnaniyat, which was the only entry from the Gulf states among 136 authors from 44 countries.
achievements: First cookbook author in Bahrain
Host of Bahrain's first cooking show
Won Best Arabic Cookbook at Gourmand World Cookbook Awards 2009
Named one of 50 Most Powerful Women in Middle East by Forbes

figures.name: Mohammed Abdulmohsen al-Kharafi
figures.title: Founder of M.A. Kharafi & Sons
figures.roleDescription: Patriarch and founder of the Al-Kharafi business empire
figures.significance: Founded M.A. Kharafi & Sons, establishing the family's business dynasty that would dominate multiple sectors of Kuwait's economy.
figures.biography: Mohammed Abdulmohsen al-Kharafi was the patriarch of the Al-Kharafi family and founder of M.A. Kharafi & Sons, which would become one of Kuwait's largest business conglomerates. His sons would go on to become prominent figures in Kuwaiti business and politics. The company he established grew to dominate several sectors of the Kuwaiti economy, including construction, telecommunications, and investment. His legacy continued through his children, who became influential leaders in their respective fields, with the family business remaining a major force in Kuwait's economic landscape for generations.
achievements: Founded M.A. Kharafi & Sons

figures.name: Jassem Al-Kharafi
figures.title: Speaker of Kuwait National Assembly
figures.roleDescription: Longtime Speaker of Parliament and business leader
figures.significance: Served as Speaker of the Kuwaiti National Assembly for five terms and played a critical role in Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah's ascension to the emirship.
figures.biography: Jassem al-Kharafi was born in Kuwait City in 1940, the son of Mohammed Abdulmohsen al-Kharafi, the founder of M.A. Kharafi & Sons. He studied Business Administration at the Manchester Trade Faculty in Kuwait and was director of M.A. Kharafi & Sons before being elected to the National Assembly in 1975. He served as Minister of Finance from 1985 to 1990. Al-Kharafi was elected Speaker of the National Assembly in 1999 and served for five terms, the most in Kuwait's history, until deciding not to stand for reelection in 2012. In his capacity as Speaker in 2006, he played a critical role in the ascension of Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah to the emirship by coordinating a no-confidence vote of the incumbent emir. During Sheikh Sabah's reign, his family conglomerate dominated several sectors of the Kuwaiti economy. He was known for his political positions, including criticism of Western provocations toward Iran and vigorous denunciation of Israeli attacks on Lebanon in 2006.
achievements: Served as Speaker of National Assembly for five terms
Minister of Finance 1985-1990
Coordinated no-confidence vote leading to Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad's ascension to emirship
Director of M.A. Kharafi & Sons

figures.name: Nasser Al-Kharafi
figures.title: Chairman and President of M.A. Kharafi & Sons
figures.roleDescription: Business magnate and one of the world's richest people
figures.significance: Ran M.A. Kharafi & Sons and was listed by Forbes as one of the richest people in the world.
figures.biography: Nasser Al-Kharafi was the brother of Jassem Al-Kharafi and ran the family's business empire, M.A. Kharafi & Sons. Under his leadership, the company became one of Kuwait's largest conglomerates, operating across multiple sectors including construction, telecommunications, and investment. His business acumen and the success of the family enterprise led to his recognition by Forbes as one of the richest people in the world. He was the father of Bader Nasser Al-Kharafi, who would later become a prominent businessman in his own right. Nasser's death in 2011 marked a significant transition for the Al-Kharafi business empire, with leadership passing to the next generation.
achievements: Listed by Forbes as one of the richest people in the world
Led M.A. Kharafi & Sons to become major Kuwaiti conglomerate

figures.name: Faiza Mohammed Al-Kharafi
figures.title: President of Kuwait University
figures.roleDescription: Chemist, academic, and first woman to head a major Middle Eastern university
figures.significance: First woman to head a major university in the Middle East and vice president of the World Academy of Sciences.
figures.biography: Faiza Mohammed Al-Kharafi was born to a wealthy family in Kuwait in 1946 and developed an interest in science from a young age. She received her BSc from Ain Shams University in Cairo in 1967, then attended Kuwait University where she founded the Corrosion and Electrochemistry Research Laboratory while in graduate school. She received her master's in 1972 and her PhD in 1975. She worked in Kuwait University's Department of Chemistry from 1975 to 1981, became chair of the department in 1984, and was Dean of the Faculty of Science from 1986 to 1989. On 5 July 1993, Emir Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah appointed her as rector of the university, making her the first woman to head a major university in the Middle East. She served as president from 1993 to 2002, overseeing 1,500 staff members and over 20,000 students. Al-Kharafi is a renowned electrochemist who studied corrosion and metal behavior, collaborated on discovering molybdenum-based catalysts, and became vice president of The World Academy of Sciences. She is married to Ali Mohammed Thanian Al-Ghanim and has five sons, including Marzouq Al-Ghanim, former speaker of Kuwait National Assembly.
achievements: First woman to head a major university in the Middle East
Vice president of the World Academy of Sciences
Founded Corrosion and Electrochemistry Research Laboratory
Kuwait Prize in Applied Sciences 2006
L'Oréal-UNESCO Award for Women in Science 2011
Named one of Forbes '100 Most Powerful Women'

figures.name: Bader Nasser Al-Kharafi
figures.title: Vice Chairman and Group CEO of Zain Group
figures.roleDescription: Telecommunications executive and business leader
figures.significance: Current leader of major telecommunications company and continues the Al-Kharafi family's business prominence in the modern era.
figures.biography: Bader Nasser Al-Kharafi was born on 17 August 1977, the son of Nasser Al-Kharafi. He received his bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering from Kuwait University in 2002 and completed his MBA from London Business School in 2016. After graduation, he joined Kuwait Petroleum Corporation and then M.A & Al-Kharafi group in 2002 as a Coordination Engineer. After his father's death in 2012, he assumed the directorship of the Al Kharafi groups' executive committee. In 2017, he became CEO of Zain Group, one of the Middle East's largest telecommunications companies, replacing Scott Gegenheimer. He currently holds senior management positions at Gulf Bank of Kuwait, Foulath Holding, Diamond International Motors, and Gulf Cable & Electrical Industries. In 2019, his company BNK Automotive entered into an agency agreement with Volvo, and he acquired a controlling interest in Gulf Cable. He has been recognized multiple times in Arabian Business's lists of most powerful and influential Arabs.
achievements: Vice Chairman and Group CEO of Zain Group
Listed in Arabian Business's 100 Most Influential Arabs
Acquired controlling interest in Gulf Cable ($500 million deal)
Exclusive Volvo Cars importer for Kuwait

figures.name: Mousa Abu Marzook
figures.title: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau
figures.roleDescription: Senior Hamas political leader and international relations chief
figures.significance: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau and key figure in Hamas's external leadership structure.
figures.biography:
Mousa Abu Marzook is a prominent Palestinian political leader who serves as Deputy Chairman of the Hamas Political Bureau and heads the group's international relations office as of 2023. He has been a central figure in Hamas's external leadership structure, operating from exile and directing operations from Damascus and later Egypt as part of what became known as the Gaza group of Hamas's external leadership.

Abu Marzook has been instrumental in Hamas's diplomatic and political activities, including participation in ceasefire negotiations during the Gaza war. In 2007, he explained Hamas's position regarding Israel, stating that while Hamas did not recognize Israel as a state, it considered Israel's existence as 'amr waqi' (fait accompli), describing this as 'de facto recognition' of Israel. He has been involved in prisoner exchange negotiations and has represented Hamas in various international forums. In early 2025, he was reportedly targeted in an Israeli airstrike on Doha along with other senior Hamas officials, though he survived the attack.
achievements: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau
Head of Hamas international relations office
Key figure in Hamas external leadership structure

figures.name: Ibrahim al-Rawas
figures.title: Baghdadi Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader who initiated restoration of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque
figures.significance: Founded the al-Rawas family's custodianship of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque and established their role in the Rifa'i Order in Baghdad.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim al-Rawas was a prominent Baghdadi sheikh from the Rifa'i Order who played a crucial role in the restoration of Islamic heritage in late 19th century Baghdad. Around 1887, he undertook a significant journey to Istanbul to meet with Ottoman Sheikh al-Islam Abu al-Huda al-Sayyadi to discuss restoring the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, which had fallen into disrepair.

His diplomatic efforts proved successful, leading to a royal decree issued by Ottoman Sultan Abdul-Hamid II years later for the mosque's renovation at a cost of 4,000 Ottoman liras. In 1892, the mosque was completely renovated under the Sultan's orders, with Ibrahim al-Rawas establishing his family's hereditary custodianship of the complex. The renovated mosque included a new madrasa overlooking al-Rashid Street, and featured appointed religious officials including an Imam, preacher, muezzin, and caretaker.

Starting with Ibrahim al-Rawas, the noble Rifa'i Baghdadi family of al-Rawas assumed custodianship of the mosque and taught in the madrasa. His initiative established a lasting legacy, as the mosque has remained under the management of the al-Rawas family since its restoration, serving as the headquarters for the Sufi Rifa'i Order in Baghdad.
achievements: Successfully negotiated with Ottoman authorities for mosque restoration
Established al-Rawas family custodianship of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque
Founded the second madrasa at the mosque complex

figures.name: Muhammad al-Rawas
figures.title: Husaynid Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Religious scholar who had a mosque built in his honor
figures.significance: Had the al-Rawas Mosque built and named after him by Sultan Abdul Hamid II, representing the family's religious prominence in Baghdad.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Muhammad al-Rawas was a respected Husaynid religious scholar who lived and worked in the Bab al-Sheikh district of Baghdad during the 19th century. He was recognized for his scholarly contributions and religious leadership within the local community, establishing the al-Rawas family's reputation for Islamic learning and piety.

Following his death in 1875, Sultan Abdul Hamid II ordered the construction of al-Rawas Mosque near al-Gilani Street in his honor in 1893. The mosque complex included both a madrasa and housed Sheikh Muhammad's tomb, demonstrating the high regard in which he was held by both the local community and Ottoman authorities. The mosque served as an important religious and educational center in the Bab al-Sheikh area.

After the mosque was demolished by Baghdad Municipality in 1954 to make way for al-Jumhuriya Street expansion, Sheikh Muhammad al-Rawas's remains were respectfully transferred to the cemetery of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, where they joined other members of the al-Rawas family. The original al-Rawas Mosque is now recognized as one of Baghdad's lost historical mosques.
achievements: Had a mosque and madrasa built in his honor by Ottoman Sultan
Established religious scholarly reputation in Bab al-Sheikh district

figures.name: Baha al-Din al-Rawas
figures.title: Rifa'i Ascetic
figures.roleDescription: Sufi mystic and ascetic of the Rifa'i Order
figures.significance: Represented the family's deep involvement in Sufi mysticism and ascetic practices within the Rifa'i Order.
figures.biography:
Baha al-Din al-Rawas was a devoted Rifa'i ascetic who dedicated his life to Sufi mysticism and spiritual practices. As a member of the prominent al-Rawas family, he embodied the family's deep connection to the Rifa'i Order and their commitment to Islamic mysticism and asceticism.

Originally, Baha al-Din was buried in his own tomb, likely connected to the original al-Rawas Mosque complex in the Bab al-Sheikh district of Baghdad. His burial site would have been a place of reverence for followers of the Rifa'i Order and members of the local community who respected his spiritual achievements and ascetic lifestyle.

When the al-Rawas Mosque was demolished in 1954, Baha al-Din's remains were carefully moved to the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, where he was reburied next to Sayyid Sultan Ali himself. This relocation honored his spiritual status and maintained the continuity of the al-Rawas family's connection to the Rifa'i Order's headquarters in Baghdad, where his tomb can still be visited today.
achievements: Achieved recognition as a Rifa'i ascetic and mystic
Maintained family tradition of Sufi spiritual leadership

figures.name: Abdullah al-Rawas
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Contemporary religious leader and mosque supervisor
figures.significance: Supervised the modern reopening of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque in 2016, continuing the family's custodial tradition.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah al-Rawas represents the contemporary generation of the al-Rawas family's religious leadership in Baghdad. As a nobleman from the Rifa'i Order, he has maintained the family's traditional role as custodians of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, continuing a responsibility that began with his ancestor Ibrahim al-Rawas in the late 19th century.

In 2016, Sheikh Abdullah al-Rawas supervised a significant reopening of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, demonstrating his active leadership in preserving and maintaining this important religious site. His involvement in this reopening ceremony highlighted the continuity of the al-Rawas family's dedication to their custodial duties despite the challenges faced by Iraq's religious institutions in recent decades.

Sheikh Abdullah's role extends beyond mere administration, as he continues the family tradition of serving the Rifa'i Order and maintaining the mosque as the headquarters for the Sufi order in Baghdad. His leadership ensures that the mosque continues to serve as both a place of worship and a center for Rifa'i spiritual activities, preserving the religious and cultural heritage that his family has protected for over a century.
achievements: Supervised the reopening of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque in 2016
Maintained family custodianship tradition into the 21st century

figures.name: Kazim al-Samawi
figures.title: Iraqi Poet and Journalist
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Iraqi poet and journalist known for his humanist worldview and exile writings
figures.significance: Known as 'The Elder of Iraqi exiles' and was a leading leftist poet who spent over half his life in political exile.
figures.biography:
Kazim Jasir Faraj, better known as Kazim al-Samawi, was born in 1925 in Samawah, Iraq. He graduated from the Rural Teachers' House in 1940 and began his literary career in the late 1940s. He rose to prominence as a left-wing realist poet and committed journalist, founding the newspaper Al-Insaniyah (The Humanity) in 1956, though it was closed in 1961.

Al-Samawi spent more than half of his life in exile as a political refugee, moving between Lebanon, Hungary, Germany, China, Syria, Cyprus, and finally Sweden. His Iraqi citizenship was revoked by Nuri al-Said's government in 1954, forcing him to seek asylum in Hungary. He witnessed the death of nearly all his family members during his years of exile, including his sons Nasir (assassinated in Beijing in 1991) and Riyad (died of cancer), his daughter Tahrir (died in London in 2008), and his wife.

He was involved in the World Peace movement and contributed to establishing the peace movement in Iraq in 1952. Al-Samawi died on 15 March 2010 in Stockholm, Sweden, at age 85. President Jalal Talabani ordered his body to be transferred to Iraq by private plane, and he was buried in Sulaymaniyah's Saiwan Cemetery as per his wishes, since he was an advocate of Iraqi Kurdish nationalism.
achievements: Founded the newspaper Al-Insaniyah (The Humanity) in 1956
Published seven poetry collections translated into several languages
Contributed to establishing the peace movement in Iraq in 1952
Represented the Arab world at the Asia and Pacific Rim Peace Conference in Beijing in 1952
Member of the Hungarian Writers' Union

figures.name: Ayla Halit Kazım
figures.title: Turkish Cypriot Politician
figures.roleDescription: First female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus
figures.significance: She was the first female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus, serving briefly in the legislature in 1963.
figures.biography:
Ayla Halit Kazım was born in Larnaca in 1934, daughter of Mehmet Zeki Müderisoğlu from Larnaca and Nezihe Efendi from Limassol. She grew up in Limassol and attended the American Academy in Larnaca. In 1953 she married Shemshedin Halit Kazım and became a farmer, settling in Paphos where they had four children.

Her husband Shemshedin became the Turkish mayor of Paphos and was elected to the House of Representatives in 1960. However, he died in a car accident in September 1963. Ayla was chosen to replace him and was elected without a by-election, taking office as the first female member on 25 October 1963. She joined the Independent Turkish Group, but alongside the other 14 Turkish Cypriot members, withdrew from the House in December 1963.

Following the 1974 coup and Turkish invasion, Kazım moved to what became Northern Cyprus, settling in Güzelyurt. She subsequently became a member of the Community Assembly of the Autonomous Turkish Cypriot Administration. In 2006 she won a court case entitling her to compensation from the Cypriot government, which had expropriated land she owned in Mandria.
achievements: First female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus
Member of the Community Assembly of the Autonomous Turkish Cypriot Administration
Won court case for land compensation in 2006

figures.name: Ahmad Bin Shafar
figures.title: Al Wasl FC Administrator
figures.roleDescription: Leader of Al Wasl Football Club administration
figures.significance: Led the new administration of Al Wasl FC starting in the 2021-22 season, helping to improve team performance and fan relations.
figures.biography:
Ahmad Bin Shafar was appointed to lead a new administration for Al Wasl Football Club for the 2021-22 season. His appointment came after a period of poor results that had led to mutual bad feeling between the club and its fans, with supporters campaigning for a change in the administration and board of directors.

Under his leadership, the club saw significant improvements in both fan relations and team performance. With the fans placated by the new administration, attendances rose, and the team's spirit and performance improved, though they only achieved 6th place in the league that season. His tenure marked the beginning of a turnaround for the club that would eventually lead to their most successful period in years.
achievements: Led Al Wasl FC administration reform
Improved fan relations and attendance

figures.name: Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil
figures.title: Family Patriarch
figures.roleDescription: Founding patriarch who led the first wave of migration from Bastak
figures.significance: Led the Al Farooq family's migration from Bastak to Dubai in 1899, establishing the family's business presence in the Gulf.
figures.biography:
Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil was the patriarch who led the Al Farooq family during the first wave of migration which started in 1899 due to the introduction of customs tax by the Persian government under the Qajar dynasty which led to reduced profits for merchants. Political instability in 1909, and a flu epidemic in 1918 saw the heads of most merchant families from Bastak relocate to the Persian Gulf region, Karachi and the Indian Ocean regions.

After Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil died, his sons Mohammed Farooq, known as The Great Farooq and Ahmed Al Arshi took over as the heads of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones. His leadership during this critical period established the foundation for what would become one of the Gulf's most prominent merchant families.
achievements: Led family migration from Bastak to Dubai in 1899
Established family's Gulf business presence

figures.name: Mohammed Farooq
figures.title: The Great Farooq
figures.roleDescription: Most famous pearl merchant and family head who expanded business internationally
figures.significance: Known as the most famous pearl merchant of Lengeh, Bastak and the Persian Gulf, whose generosity earned him respect among Gulf royalty.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Farooq, known as The Great Farooq, was the son of Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil. After his father died, Mohammed Farooq took over as the head of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones. Today Mohammed Farooq is remembered as the most famous pearl merchant of Lengeh, Bastak and the Persian Gulf.

As Coles and Jakson put it, 'The Great Farooq's generosity and hospitality earned him respect and a reputable status amongst Gulf royalty.' He had close ties with the rulers of Arab States of the Persian Gulf, namely the late Sheikh Mohammed Bin Saqer Al Qasimi, the former ruler of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah and the late Sheikh Saeed Bin Maktoum Al Maktoum, The former Ruler of Dubai. His daughter Faiqa Farooq became the Arab world's first female ambassador of the United Nations.
achievements: Most famous pearl merchant of the Persian Gulf
Built close relationships with Gulf royalty
Expanded family business internationally

figures.name: Abdulkarim Farooq
figures.title: Family Heir and Advisor
figures.roleDescription: Heir to the family business and advisor to Gulf rulers
figures.significance: Regarded as the wealthiest man in Dubai, he owned the London Store and served as advisor to former rulers across the Persian Gulf.
figures.biography:
After Mohammed Farooq died, his son Abdulkarim Farooq became the heir to the family. Abdulkarim Farooq was also an advisor to the former rulers across the Persian Gulf, continuing his father's tradition of close relationships with regional leadership.

Abdulkarim Farooq owned a general trading establishment in Al Bastakiya, Dubai known as London store which specialized in importing quality brands directly from London, UK. He was regarded as the 'wealthiest man in Dubai and owned symbols of affluence which included a yacht.' Like his father 'The Great Farooq' before him, Abdulkarim Farooq was an extremely close associate and advisor to the rulers of the Gulf.
achievements: Owned the London Store in Al Bastakiya
Regarded as wealthiest man in Dubai
Served as advisor to Gulf rulers

figures.name: Ahmed Al Arshi
figures.title: Co-head of Family Business
figures.roleDescription: Joint leader of the family alongside Mohammed Farooq
figures.significance: Co-headed the Al Farooq family business alongside Mohammed Farooq, expanding operations internationally in diamonds, pearls and precious stones.
figures.biography:
Ahmed Al Arshi was the son of Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil and brother to Mohammed Farooq (The Great Farooq). After their father Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil died, his sons Mohammed Farooq and Ahmed Al Arshi took over as the heads of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones.

Ahmed Al Arshi worked alongside his more famous brother in building the family's international trading empire. The family branches are closely related, with the Farooq Al Arshi family being first cousins of the Al Olema family, also known as the Sheikh Sultan Al Ulama, the Fikree, and the Al Khaldi family.
achievements: Co-headed family business expansion
Established international trading operations

figures.name: Faiqa Farooq
figures.title: UN Ambassador
figures.roleDescription: Arab world's first female ambassador to the United Nations
figures.significance: Became the Arab world's first female ambassador to the United Nations, breaking gender barriers in Middle Eastern diplomacy.
figures.biography:
Faiqa Farooq, daughter of The Great Farooq, was the Arab world's first female ambassador of the United Nations. Faiqa was born in Paris in 1929, and joined the diplomatic corps of Tunisia due to her Tunisian mother, and was soon posted to Washington as the Third Secretary.

The female members of the Farooq family have contributed successfully to various fields, with Faiqa being the most prominent example. Her appointment as the first female Arab ambassador to the UN represented a significant milestone for women in Middle Eastern diplomacy and reflected the progressive values of the Al Farooq family.
achievements: First Arab woman ambassador to the United Nations
Served as Third Secretary in Washington

figures.name: Dr. Mohammed Zubair Farooq Al Arshi
figures.title: Doctor and Poet
figures.roleDescription: Renowned medical doctor and poet representing the modern generation
figures.significance: A renowned poet and medical doctor who represents the family's continued prominence in professional and cultural fields.
figures.biography:
Dr. Mohammed Zubair Farooq Al Arshi is identified as the son of Mohammed Aqil Mohammed Farooq Mohammed Aqil Arshi, making him part of the contemporary generation of the Al Farooq family. He has achieved recognition as both a renowned poet and medical doctor, representing the family's continued success across multiple professional fields.

As the rapidly expanding family business has become a truly global brand through various members of the respectable family, Dr. Mohammed Zubair represents the intellectual and cultural contributions of the newer generation, maintaining the family's reputation for excellence while pursuing careers in medicine and literature.
achievements: Renowned poet and medical doctor
Represents contemporary generation of family

figures.name: Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq
figures.title: Current Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Current leader of family business operations
figures.significance: Currently leads the family business alongside other family members, continuing the entrepreneurial and philanthropic traditions.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq is one of the current leaders of the Al Farooq family business. As the son of Abdulkarim Farooq, he represents the continuing leadership of the family's commercial enterprises into the modern era.

Family members that currently rule the business include Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq, Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and Rashid Farooq. The family is profoundly known for their ambitious, entrepreneurial and philanthropist stance and work with many international, commercial and charitable organisations, maintaining the traditions established by previous generations.
achievements: Current leader of family business
Continues entrepreneurial and philanthropic traditions

figures.name: Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq
figures.title: Current Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Co-leader of contemporary family business operations
figures.significance: Co-leads the modern Al Farooq business operations, maintaining the family's prominence in international commerce.
figures.biography:
Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq is another son of Abdulkarim Farooq and serves as one of the current leaders of the Al Farooq family business. He works alongside his brother Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and cousin Rashid Farooq in managing the family's diverse business interests.

The family members that currently rule the business including Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq, Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and Rashid Farooq continue the family's reputation for ambitious, entrepreneurial and philanthropist activities, working with many international, commercial and charitable organisations around the world.
achievements: Co-leader of current family business
Maintains international business relationships

figures.name: Rashid Farooq
figures.title: Current Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Contemporary family business leader
figures.significance: Part of the current leadership trio managing the Al Farooq family's global business operations.
figures.biography:
Rashid Farooq is one of the three current leaders of the Al Farooq family business, working alongside Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq. He represents the contemporary generation that has transformed the family business into a truly global brand.

Family members that currently rule the business including Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq, Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and Rashid Farooq are known for their ambitious, entrepreneurial and philanthropist stance. They work with many international, commercial and charitable organisations, continuing the family's long tradition of business success and social responsibility.
achievements: Current family business leader
Maintains global business operations

figures.name: Abdul Latif Jameel
figures.title: Founder and Business Pioneer
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Abdul Latif Jameel company and Toyota distributor in Saudi Arabia
figures.significance: Founded the eponymous company in 1945 and acquired distribution rights to Toyota vehicles in Saudi Arabia in 1955, establishing one of the region's major business empires.
figures.biography:
Abdul Latif Jameel (1909-1993) was a Saudi businessman who founded the Abdul Latif Jameel company in 1945. His most significant business achievement came in 1955 when he acquired distribution rights to Toyota vehicles in Saudi Arabia, a move that would establish his company as one of the largest independent distributors of Toyota and Lexus vehicles in the world.

Jameel's business acumen and early partnership with Toyota created the foundation for what would become a major international conglomerate. His company expanded beyond automotive distribution into various sectors, building a diversified family-owned business empire. The success of his initial ventures with Toyota distribution rights proved to be transformative, positioning the Abdul Latif Jameel company as a significant player in the Saudi Arabian and regional business landscape.

His legacy continues through his descendants, who have maintained and expanded the business while also establishing major philanthropic initiatives. The family name has become associated with numerous educational and research institutions worldwide, including MIT's Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab and various other academic collaborations.
achievements: Founded Abdul Latif Jameel company in 1945
Acquired Toyota distribution rights for Saudi Arabia in 1955
Established one of the world's largest independent Toyota and Lexus distributorship networks

figures.name: Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel
figures.title: Chairman and President
figures.roleDescription: Chairman and President of Abdul Latif Jameel company
figures.significance: Led the Abdul Latif Jameel company as the second generation, overseeing its continued growth and expansion as an international conglomerate.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel is the son of founder Abdul Latif Jameel and serves as chairman and president of the Abdul Latif Jameel company. As the second generation leader of the family business, he has overseen the company's continued growth and diversification beyond its original Toyota distribution roots.

Under his leadership, the company has maintained its position as one of the largest independent distributors of Toyota and Lexus vehicles globally while expanding into other business sectors. Mohammed has been instrumental in maintaining the family's business empire and ensuring its continued success across multiple generations. His leadership has helped preserve the company's strong relationship with Toyota while exploring new business opportunities and markets.
achievements: Chairman and President of Abdul Latif Jameel company
Continued expansion of family business empire
Maintained strong partnership with Toyota Motor Corporation

figures.name: Hassan Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel
figures.title: Deputy President and Vice Chairman
figures.roleDescription: Deputy President and Vice Chairman of Saudi Arabia operations at Abdul Latif Jameel
figures.significance: Third generation leader who has modernized the family business through strategic investments in electric vehicles and expanded the family's philanthropic efforts globally.
figures.biography:
Hassan Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel was born in 1979 or 1980 and is a Saudi businessman and philanthropist representing the third generation of the Abdul Latif Jameel family business. He is the middle son of Mohammed Abdul Latif Jameel and grandson of founder Abdul Latif Jameel. Hassan received an international education, attending high school in Japan before earning a Bachelor of Arts in international economics from Sophia University in Tokyo in 2001, followed by an MBA from the London Business School.

His career began with training in the domestic kaizen department of Toyota Motor Corporation in Japan in 2004, strengthening the family's long-standing relationship with Toyota. He currently serves as deputy president and vice chairman of domestic Saudi Arabia operations at Abdul Latif Jameel. In 2011, Hassan made a pioneering investment when he met with R.J. Scaringe, founder of electric vehicle startup Rivian, and convinced him to produce an electric pickup truck. Abdul Latif Jameel became Rivian's largest investor, and Hassan joined the company's board of directors.

Beyond business, Hassan is deeply involved in philanthropy as vice chairman of Community Jameel, an international philanthropic organization founded in 2003 that promotes social and economic initiatives including refugee education, job creation, poverty alleviation, and healthcare improvement. He is also involved in humanitarian programs with the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees and is an art collector and supporter of the arts. Hassan is fluent in Arabic, English, and Japanese.
achievements: Deputy President and Vice Chairman of Abdul Latif Jameel Saudi Arabia operations
Led major investment in electric vehicle company Rivian
Vice Chairman of Community Jameel philanthropic organization
Board member of Rivian
Fluent in three languages (Arabic, English, Japanese)

figures.name: Fady Jameel
figures.title: President of Community Jameel
figures.roleDescription: President of Community Jameel philanthropic organization
figures.significance: Co-leads the family's major philanthropic initiatives and has been instrumental in establishing collaborations with prestigious academic institutions worldwide.
figures.biography:
Fady Jameel serves as President of Community Jameel, the Abdul Latif Jameel family's international philanthropic organization. Along with Hassan Jameel, he has been a key figure in launching major academic and research collaborations that bear the family name. In October 2019, he participated in the signing ceremony that established the Abdul Latif Jameel Institute for Disease and Emergency Analytics at Imperial College London, alongside Imperial's president Alice Gast and Hassan Jameel.

Fady was also present at the September 2018 founding of the MIT Abdul Latif Jameel Clinic for Machine Learning in Health, participating in the signing ceremony at MIT with MIT President L. Rafael Reif and Hassan Jameel. His leadership in Community Jameel has been crucial in establishing the organization as a major force in international philanthropy, supporting initiatives in health, education, poverty alleviation, and scientific research across multiple continents.
achievements: President of Community Jameel
Co-founded Abdul Latif Jameel Institute at Imperial College London
Co-founded MIT Abdul Latif Jameel Clinic for Machine Learning in Health
Leader in international philanthropic initiatives

figures.name: Mohammed Jameel
figures.title: Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Saudi business leader and philanthropist
figures.significance: Provided the endowment that established J-PAL at MIT in 2005, transforming it from the 'Poverty Action Lab' to the 'Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab.'
figures.biography: Mohammed Jameel of Saudi Arabia's Abdul Latif Jameel Corporation played a crucial role in the development of one of the world's most influential development economics research centers. In 2005, he provided the endowment that allowed MIT's economics department to rename and expand the 'Poverty Action Lab' to become the 'Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL).'
achievements: Endowed J-PAL at MIT in 2005
Supported major academic research in development economics

figures.name: Salem Bin Mahfouz
figures.title: Founder of National Commercial Bank
figures.roleDescription: Saudi entrepreneur and banking pioneer
figures.significance: Founded the first private Saudi bank and became personal banker to the Saudi royal family.
figures.biography: Salem Ahmed bin Mahfouz was a Saudi entrepreneur who rose from being an illiterate small-time money-changer to becoming the founder of the National Commercial Bank (NCB), the first private bank in Saudi Arabia. He established himself as a significant figure in Saudi banking and finance, eventually becoming the personal banker of the Saudi royal family. His success in banking laid the foundation for one of Saudi Arabia's most prominent business families. Salem handed management of NCB, which became the largest bank in the country, to his son Khalid sometime in the 1980s, establishing a banking dynasty that would continue to influence Saudi finance for decades.
achievements: Founded the National Commercial Bank, the first private Saudi bank
Became personal banker to the Saudi royal family
Built one of Saudi Arabia's most prominent business families

figures.name: Khalid bin Mahfouz
figures.title: Chairman of National Commercial Bank
figures.roleDescription: Saudi billionaire banker and businessman
figures.significance: Saudi billionaire banker who served as chairman of NCB and faced post-9/11 scrutiny over alleged terrorism financing.
figures.biography:
Khalid bin Mahfouz was born on December 26, 1949, as the second eldest son of Salem Ahmed bin Mahfouz. He inherited management of the National Commercial Bank (NCB) from his father sometime in the 1980s, becoming chairman of Saudi Arabia's largest bank. With a personal wealth estimated around $6 billion, he ranked 24th in Arabian Business magazine's list of the world's most influential Arabs in 2008 and number 214 in Forbes Billionaires List the same year. In the 1970s, he purchased a $3.5 million chateaux-style house in Houston's River Oaks area and acquired a 44,000-acre ranch in Liberty County, Texas. He obtained Irish citizenship through investment procedures in 1990.

After the September 11 attacks, Khalid faced considerable scrutiny as investigators examined Saudi financing of terrorism. He was accused in various publications of funneling money to Al Qaeda, though he denied these allegations. He had been a non-executive director of Bank of Credit and Commerce International (BCCI) with a 20% stake, and was later indicted for fraud, settling for $225 million in 1995. He acknowledged making one contribution of $270,000 to support the US-sponsored resistance against Soviet occupation of Afghanistan. Khalid died on August 16, 2009, leaving behind a complex legacy as both a prominent banker and a figure controversially linked to terrorism financing allegations.
achievements: Built NCB into Saudi Arabia's largest bank
Accumulated personal wealth of approximately $6 billion
Ranked among the world's most influential Arabs and Forbes billionaires

figures.name: Abdulrahman bin Mahfouz
figures.title: Board Director
figures.roleDescription: Son of Khalid bin Mahfouz and board member of Muwafaq Foundation
figures.significance: Served on the board of directors of the Muwafaq Foundation charity organization established by his father.
figures.biography: Abdulrahman bin Mahfouz is the eldest son of Saudi billionaire banker Khalid bin Mahfouz. He was appointed to serve on the board of directors of the Muwafaq Foundation, a charity organization that his father helped establish and funded with $30 million. The foundation, whose name means 'blessed relief' in Arabic, was later designated by the U.S. Treasury Department in October 2001 as a front organization, though neither Abdulrahman nor his father were directly accused of funding terrorism by the United States authorities. His role in the foundation represented the family's involvement in charitable activities, though it later became subject to scrutiny in the post-9/11 investigations into terrorism financing.

figures.name: Mahfouz Marei Mubarak bin Mahfouz
figures.title: Saudi Arabian Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Businessman and philanthropist with ties to British institutions
figures.significance: Saudi businessman involved in philanthropy and British institutions, later subject to cash-for-honors allegations.
figures.biography:
Mahfouz Marei Mubarak bin Mahfouz was born on December 14, 1969, in Mecca, Saudi Arabia. He became a prominent businessman with extensive connections to British institutions and charitable organizations. He was made a Foundation Fellow at Pembroke College, Oxford in 2012 and became the inaugural Bredon Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge in July 2013. The Mahfouz Building at Pembroke College, Oxford was named after him, as was a room at Wolfson College, Cambridge. He was admitted to the Freedom of the City of London in December 2013 and served as a Vice-President of the British Forces Foundation.

Mahfouz became involved with Prince Charles's charitable organizations, serving as an Ambassador and Community Patron for The Prince's Foundation. He donated over £1.5 million to royal charities and met with Prince Harry in 2013 and 2014, contributing £50,000 to Harry's charity Sentebale and £10,000 to Walking With The Wounded. However, Harry stated in 2021 that he severed ties with Mahfouz in 2015 after expressing 'growing concerns' about his motives. Mahfouz was awarded an honorary Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in 2016 'for services to charities in the UK,' though this later became subject to cash-for-honors allegations involving claims that the honor was obtained through donations rather than merit.
achievements: Made Foundation Fellow at Pembroke College, Oxford (2012)
Became inaugural Bredon Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge (2013)
Awarded honorary Commander of the Order of the British Empire (2016)
Admitted to the Freedom of the City of London (2013)

figures.name: Safeya Binzagr
figures.title: Artist and Museum Founder
figures.roleDescription: Saudi Arabian artist and museum owner who established the first private art museum in Saudi Arabia
figures.significance: She was the only artist in Saudi Arabia to have their own museum and was a pioneering figure in Saudi contemporary art.
figures.biography:
Safeya Binzagr was born in 1940 to a well-known merchant family in Jeddah. She was privately taught art in Egypt and went on to earn a degree from St Martin's School of Art in 1965. Her artistic journey began with her first exhibition in 1968, and in 1970, she became the first woman to hold a solo exhibition of her work in Saudi Arabia. Despite her groundbreaking achievements, Binzagr was not allowed to attend the openings of her own exhibitions until Aramco held a private exhibition of her work in 1976, being represented instead by male members of her family.

Binzagr's artistic work used various mediums, ranging from oil paint, watercolor, pastel, drawing and etchings, often centering around daily life in Saudi Arabia. She created series of works based on themes such as marriage customs, local costumes and old homes in Saudi Arabia, painting cultural themes to preserve the cultural traditions of her country. In 1973, she chose to stop selling her art, and in 1979, she published a book about Saudi Arabian art called 'Saudi Arabia, An Artist's View of the Past,' which was translated into English and French.

In 1989, she began planning what would become her greatest legacy - a permanent space to display and curate her work. After nine years of planning and construction, the Darat Safeya Binzagr opened in 2000, serving as her home, studio, and gallery. The museum offered free admission and became a cultural hub where Binzagr hosted public events to promote art in Saudi Arabia. She died on September 12, 2024, at the age of 84.
achievements: First woman to hold a solo art exhibition in Saudi Arabia (1970)
Only artist in Saudi Arabia to have their own museum
Founded Darat Safeya Binzagr museum in 2000
Published 'Saudi Arabia, An Artist's View of the Past' (1979)
Pioneered cultural preservation through art depicting Saudi traditions

figures.name: Shalimar Sharbatly
figures.title: Abstract Painter
figures.roleDescription: Saudi Arabian abstract painter
figures.significance: Notable Saudi Arabian artist specializing in abstract painting.
figures.biography:
Shalimar Sharbatly was born in 1971 and is recognized as a prominent figure in contemporary Saudi Arabian art. She is listed among the notable Saudi Arabian artists and is specifically known for her work in abstract painting. Her inclusion in the comprehensive list of Saudi Arabian artists demonstrates her significance in the kingdom's artistic landscape.

As part of the contemporary generation of Saudi artists, Sharbatly represents the growing recognition of Saudi Arabian visual arts on both national and international levels. Her specialization in abstract painting places her within the modern artistic movements that have emerged in Saudi Arabia as the country has undergone cultural and social transformations.

figures.name: Princess Fahdah Hashim (born Fahdah Mohammed Abunayyan)
figures.title: Princess of Jordan
figures.roleDescription: Saudi-born member of Jordanian royal family through marriage
figures.significance: Notable member of the Abunayyan family who married into the Jordanian royal family as wife of Prince Hashim bin Hussein.
figures.biography:
Princess Fahdah Hashim was born Fahdah Mohammed Abunayyan in Saudi Arabia. She is the daughter of Sheikh Mohammad bin Ibrahim bun Suleiman Abuniyyan and her maternal grandfather is Sheikh Turki bin Khaled bin Ahmed Al Sudairi, who served as head of the Saudi Human Rights Commission. On 6 January 2006, she became engaged to Prince Hashim bin Hussein, the younger son of King Hussein of Jordan and Queen Noor.

On 15 April 2006, she married Prince Hashim bin Hussein in Jordan, becoming a member of the Jordanian royal family. The couple has five children together: three daughters and two sons born between 2007 and 2019. Their children are Princess Haalah bint Hashim (born 6 April 2007), Princess Rayet Al-Noor bint Hashim (born 4 July 2008), Princess Fatima Al-Alia bint Hashim (born 5 November 2011), Prince Hussein Haidara bin Hashim (born 15 June 2015), and Prince Mohammad Al-Hassan bin Hashim (born 21 October 2019). On 1 June 2023, she attended the Wedding of Hussein, Crown Prince of Jordan, and Rajwa Al Saif.
achievements: Married into Jordanian royal family
Mother of five royal children

figures.name: Sheikh Mohammad bin Ibrahim bun Suleiman Abuniyyan
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Father of Princess Fahdah Hashim
figures.significance: Head of the Abunayyan family whose daughter married into the Jordanian royal family.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Mohammad bin Ibrahim bun Suleiman Abuniyyan is the father of Princess Fahdah Hashim (born Fahdah Mohammed Abunayyan). He represents the Abunayyan merchant family from Saudi Arabia. His daughter's marriage to Prince Hashim bin Hussein in 2006 created a connection between the Abunayyan family and the Jordanian royal family.

Through his daughter's marriage, Sheikh Mohammad became connected to the Hashemite royal family of Jordan. His daughter has given birth to five royal grandchildren, continuing the family lineage within the Jordanian monarchy.

figures.name: Muhammad Abdullah Rashid Abunayyan
figures.title: Chairman of the Board of Directors
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of ACWA Power company of Saudi Arabia
figures.significance: Leading business figure from the Abunayyan family who heads a major renewable energy company.
figures.biography:
Muhammad Abdullah Rashid Abunayyan serves as Chairman of the Board of Directors of ACWA Power, a leading Saudi Arabian company in the renewable energy sector. He has been involved in high-level discussions regarding energy cooperation and renewable energy development in the region.

In 2023, he held meetings with Mikayil Jabbarov, Azerbaijan's Minister of Economy, to discuss attracting investors to Azerbaijan's energy sector, boosting the efficiency of renewable energy, and increasing the share of alternative energy sources. These discussions highlighted the important role of renewable energy in economic development strategies and emphasized cooperation with ACWA Power as one of the leading companies in the renewable energy sector.
achievements: Chairman of major renewable energy company ACWA Power
Involved in international energy cooperation discussions

figures.name: Ramez Jeraisy
figures.title: Mayor of Nazareth
figures.roleDescription: Mayor who collaborated on industrial development projects
figures.significance: Mayor of Nazareth who worked with Israeli industrialist Stef Wertheimer to establish an industrial park promoting Arab-Jewish coexistence.
figures.biography:
Ramez Jeraisy served as Mayor of Nazareth, the mixed Muslim-Christian Arab city in Israel. He collaborated with Israeli industrialist Stef Wertheimer on establishing an industrial park in Nazareth that opened in April 2013. The project was designed as a unique model to promote the advancement of Arab-Jewish Israeli export companies, with Jews and Arabs working side by side.

In 2009, Jeraisy met with Pope Benedict XVI alongside Wertheimer at the site of the future industrial park, where the Pope gave his blessing to the project. The industrial park was part of Wertheimer's broader vision of using industry to create jobs and foster coexistence in the region, following five principles: exports, education, coexistence, community and culture.
achievements: Established industrial park in Nazareth promoting Arab-Jewish coexistence
Met with Pope Benedict XVI to receive blessing for the industrial development project

figures.name: Mohammed Mani Ahmad al-Qahtani
figures.title: Former Guantanamo Detainee
figures.roleDescription: Saudi citizen detained as alleged al-Qaeda operative
figures.significance: Alleged 20th hijacker for September 11 attacks who was detained at Guantanamo Bay for 20 years.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Mani Ahmad al-Qahtani was born on November 19, 1975, in Kharj, Saudi Arabia, to a large Sunni family. His father served as a police officer for 28 years, and his mother raised their twelve children. He has seven brothers and four sisters. On August 3, 2001, at age 25, Qahtani flew into Orlando, Florida, from Dubai but was refused entry by immigration agent José Meléndez-Pérez, who was suspicious that he intended to become an illegal immigrant. He was sent back to Dubai and subsequently returned to Saudi Arabia.

Captured in the Battle of Tora Bora in December 2001, Qahtani was shipped to the Guantanamo Bay detention camp in June 2002. After ten months, U.S. authorities discovered through fingerprints that he was the same person who had tried to enter the United States before the September 11 attacks. He was subjected to enhanced interrogation techniques authorized by Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld, including isolation, sleep deprivation, forced nudity, and exposure to cold. In 2009, Susan Crawford of the Department of Defense admitted 'we tortured Qahtani,' marking the first official admission of torture at Guantanamo. On March 6, 2022, Qahtani was released and flown back to Saudi Arabia to a mental health treatment facility after 20 years in American custody.

figures.name: Saud bin Abdullah al-Qahtani
figures.title: Former Royal Court Advisor
figures.roleDescription: Saudi consultant and former royal court advisor
figures.significance: Former advisor to King Abdullah and Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman who oversaw the killing of Jamal Khashoggi.
figures.biography:
Saud bin Abdullah al-Qahtani was born in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, on July 7, 1978. He received a bachelor's degree in law from King Saud University, graduated with the rank of sergeant from the Royal Saudi Air Force, and obtained a master's degree in criminal justice from the Naif Arab University for Security Sciences. After obtaining his law degree, al-Qahtani taught law-related courses at security colleges in Saudi Arabia and worked as a journalist for Elaph and contributor for Al Riyadh. In 2003, he served as a legal advisor to the secretariat to then crown prince Abdullah bin Abdul Aziz, and was officially appointed an advisor to the royal court in 2012, given the rank of minister in 2015.

Al-Qahtani served as General Supervisor of the Center for Studies and Media Affairs and was described as a 'nationalist ideologue' who served as a major influence over Mohammed bin Salman's foreign and domestic policies. He was instrumental in the 2017 Saudi Arabian purge, luring targets to The Ritz-Carlton by arranging fake meetings, and reportedly oversaw the torture of several female activists in 2018. The United States intelligence community identified al-Qahtani as the ringleader of the assassination of Saudi dissident Jamal Khashoggi in October 2018. After the assassination, he was dismissed as the royal court's supervisor of media affairs, sanctioned by the US Treasury, and placed under house arrest. Turkish officials issued a warrant for his arrest, though Saudi Arabia rejected Turkey's extradition request.
achievements: Served as advisor to King Abdullah
Appointed General Supervisor of the Center for Studies and Media Affairs
Given ministerial rank in 2015

figures.name: Mohammad Fahad Muflih al-Qahtani
figures.title: Human Rights Activist
figures.roleDescription: Economics professor and human rights activist
figures.significance: Co-founder and leader of the Saudi Civil and Political Rights Association who was imprisoned for his human rights activism.
figures.biography:
Mohammad Fahad Muflih al-Qahtani was born in 1965 and has a PhD from Indiana University Bloomington in the United States. As of June 2012, he was employed as an economics professor at the Institute of Diplomatic Affairs of the Saudi Arabian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Al-Qahtani co-founded the Saudi Civil and Political Rights Association (ACPRA) in October 2009 together with Mohammed Saleh al-Bejadi and nine others, and continued to be active in the organization as of June 2012. In November 2008, he was among 20 human rights activists who started a two-day hunger strike protesting the imprisonment without fair trials of 11 activists.

Al-Qahtani was charged in a Saudi court on June 18, 2012, on 11 charges related to his human rights activism, including setting up an unlicensed organization, breaking allegiance to the ruler, and accusing the judiciary of allowing torture. On March 9, 2013, he was sentenced to ten years in prison followed by ten years of travel ban. He was shifted into solitary confinement on December 17, 2018, and carried out several hunger strikes in 2020 and 2021 in protest against prison conditions. In 2018, he was awarded the Right Livelihood Award together with other jailed activists Abdullah al-Hamid and Waleed Abulkhair. Al-Qahtani was released from prison on January 7, 2025, but was given a 10-year travel ban preventing him from visiting his family in the US.
achievements: Co-founded Saudi Civil and Political Rights Association
Awarded Right Livelihood Award in 2018
PhD from Indiana University Bloomington

figures.name: Muhammad Abdullah al-Qahtani
figures.title: Self-proclaimed Mahdi
figures.roleDescription: Spiritual leader of the Grand Mosque seizure
figures.significance: Self-proclaimed Mahdi who was declared the redeemer by insurgents during the 1979 Grand Mosque seizure in Mecca.
figures.biography:
Muhammad Abdullah al-Qahtani was the brother-in-law of Juhayman al-Otaybi, who declared him to be the Mahdi, or redeemer, believed to arrive on earth several years before Judgment Day. His followers emphasized that Al-Qahtani's name and his father's name were identical to the Prophet Muhammad's name and that of his father, developing the saying 'His and his father's names were the same as Mohammed's and his father's, and he had come to Makkah from the north' to justify their belief. Al-Qahtani and al-Otaybi had met while imprisoned together for sedition, when al-Otaybi claimed to have had a vision sent by God telling him that Qahtani was the Mahdi.

Their declared goal was to institute a theocracy in preparation for the imminent apocalypse, differing from earlier Wahhabi purists in that they were millenarians who rejected the monarchy and condemned the Wahhabi ulama. The date of their attack on the Grand Mosque, November 20, 1979, was the last day of the year 1399 according to the Islamic calendar, tying in with the tradition of the mujaddid, a person who appears at the turn of every century to revive Islam. Al-Qahtani was killed during the Saudi forces' recapture of the Grand Mosque, which lasted more than two weeks and officially left 255 people dead.

figures.name: Marafi Behbehani
figures.title: Merchant
figures.roleDescription: Early merchant settler and founder of Behbehani commercial presence in Kuwait
figures.significance: One of the first merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century, establishing the Behbehani family's commercial presence.
figures.biography:
Marafi Behbehani was one of the first merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century, establishing what would become one of Kuwait's most prominent merchant families. He played a crucial role in Kuwait's early commercial development during the pre-oil era when the territory was establishing itself as a trading port. The arms trade was exclusively under the domain of Ajam merchants like Behbehani, indicating his significant role in Kuwait's early economy.

The Behbehani family, through Marafi's early settlement, became part of Kuwait's foundational merchant class that helped establish the country's commercial infrastructure. His descendants would go on to maintain substantial economic influence in Kuwait, particularly through the Behbehani and Marafi family branches that continued to play important roles in Kuwaiti business and society.
achievements: Established Behbehani family commercial presence in Kuwait
Participated in exclusive arms trade business

figures.name: Yusuf Behbehani
figures.title: Hotel Pioneer
figures.roleDescription: Builder of Kuwait's first hotel
figures.significance: Built the first hotel in Kuwait City, pioneering the hospitality industry in pre-oil Kuwait.
figures.biography:
Yusuf Behbehani was a pioneering figure in Kuwait's hospitality industry during the pre-oil era. He constructed the first hotel in Kuwait City, marking a significant milestone in the country's commercial and tourism development. This achievement demonstrated the entrepreneurial spirit of the Behbehani family and their contribution to modernizing Kuwait's infrastructure.

As part of the Ajam community, Behbehani represented the innovative contributions that Persian Kuwaitis made to the country's development. His hotel likely served the growing number of merchants, traders, and visitors who came to Kuwait as it established itself as an important trading hub in the Persian Gulf region.
achievements: Built the first hotel in Kuwait City

figures.name: Murad Behbehani
figures.title: Media Pioneer
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Kuwait Television
figures.significance: First person to officially introduce television to Kuwait and founded Kuwait Television (KTV) before it was nationalized.
figures.biography:
Murad Behbehani was a pioneering figure in Kuwait's media landscape, credited as the first person to officially introduce television to the country. He founded Kuwait Television (KTV), which would become the cornerstone of Kuwait's broadcasting industry. His vision and entrepreneurial initiative helped establish Kuwait as a leader in Gulf Arab media and entertainment.

Behbehani's television company was later nationalized by the Kuwaiti government, reflecting the strategic importance of the media industry he had helped create. His contribution to Kuwait's media development was part of the broader pattern of innovation and modernization that the Behbehani family brought to various sectors of Kuwaiti society during the country's rapid development in the mid-20th century.
achievements: Founded Kuwait Television (KTV)
First to officially introduce television to Kuwait

figures.name: Kazem Behbehani
figures.title: Dr.
figures.roleDescription: Immunologist, professor, and WHO official
figures.significance: Distinguished immunologist and retired professor who served as a World Health Organization official.
figures.biography:
Dr. Kazem Behbehani is a distinguished member of the Behbehani family who achieved international recognition in the field of medicine and public health. He served as an immunologist and professor, contributing significantly to medical research and education. His career included important work with the World Health Organization (WHO), where he held official positions and contributed to global health initiatives.

As a retired professor, Dr. Behbehani represents the intellectual and academic achievements of the Behbehani family in modern Kuwait. His work in immunology and his service with the WHO demonstrate the family's continued contribution to Kuwaiti society and international affairs, moving beyond their traditional commercial roots into professional and academic fields.
achievements: Served as World Health Organization official
Distinguished career in immunology
Academic professor

figures.name: Shafik Wazzan
figures.title: Former Prime Minister of Lebanon
figures.roleDescription: Lebanese political leader who served as Prime Minister
figures.significance: Former Prime Minister of Lebanon who survived a car bomb attack in late 1991.
figures.biography: Shafik Wazzan served as Prime Minister of Lebanon during the tumultuous period of the Lebanese Civil War. He was a prominent Sunni Muslim political figure who held the highest executive office in the country. In late December 1991, Wazzan was the target of a significant assassination attempt when a car bomb, estimated at 220 pounds of TNT, exploded in the Muslim neighborhood of Basta. The attack killed at least thirty people and wounded 120 others. Remarkably, Wazzan survived the attack because he was riding in a bulletproof car at the time of the explosion. This incident occurred during the post-civil war period when Lebanon was still experiencing sporadic violence despite the official end of hostilities in 1990. The attack on Wazzan highlighted the continued dangers facing Lebanese political leaders even after the implementation of the amnesty law in March 1991.
achievements: Served as Prime Minister of Lebanon
Survived assassination attempt in 1991

figures.name: Shams al-Mulk Abu'l-Fath Tahir ibn Wazzan
figures.nameAr: شمس الملك أبو الفتح طاهر بن وزان
figures.title: Former Vizier of the Fatimid Caliphate
figures.roleDescription: High-ranking administrative official in the Fatimid government
figures.significance: Served as vizier of the Fatimid Caliphate before being replaced by al-Rudhabari in 1024.
figures.biography: Shams al-Mulk Abu'l-Fath Tahir ibn Wazzan held the prestigious position of vizier in the Fatimid Caliphate during the early 11th century. The vizierate was one of the most important administrative positions in the Fatimid state, serving as the chief minister to the caliph and overseeing the various government departments (diwans). Ibn Wazzan's tenure as vizier came to an end in 1024 during the reign of Caliph al-Zahir (r. 1021–1036). He was replaced by al-Rudhabari, who was appointed under the general direction of the diwan. The Fatimid vizierate during this period was characterized by frequent changes in leadership, often reflecting the political instability and court intrigues that marked al-Zahir's reign. Ibn Wazzan's dismissal was part of the broader administrative reorganization that occurred during the early years of al-Zahir's caliphate, as the young caliph and his advisors sought to consolidate power and manage the various challenges facing the Fatimid state.
achievements: Served as vizier of the Fatimid Caliphate
Held high administrative position during al-Zahir's reign

figures.name: Fahed Faisal Boodai
figures.title: Chairman and Co-founder of Gatehouse Financial Group
figures.roleDescription: Kuwaiti entrepreneur and investment banker
figures.significance: Co-founder and chairman of Gatehouse Financial Group and Gatehouse Bank, pioneering force in the UK's Build to Rent sector.
figures.biography:
Fahed Faisal Boodai is a Kuwaiti entrepreneur who received an MBA from Loyola Marymount University in Los Angeles and a bachelor's degree in International Business from the University of San Diego. When Iraq invaded Kuwait in 1990, Boodai was 17 years old and joined the Kuwait armed forces in exile, undergoing basic training and working as a translator with the US military due to his fluency in English and Arabic. He was posted to military intelligence with the 18th Airborne Corps operating on the northern border of Saudi Arabia and later helped the US Army's host nation affairs unit foster relations with the Kuwaiti population after liberation.

Boodai began his career as deputy manager of the International Division at Securities House, working his way up to vice chairman and chief executive until June 2023. In 2002, he founded Gatehouse Capital KSCC, a Kuwaiti investment firm, where he led strategic investments in student housing, single family rentals in the US and UK, and over US$1.25 billion in US Light Industrial projects. In 2007, he co-founded Gatehouse Bank, leading its restructuring into real estate and wealth management in 2011 and later into a digital challenger bank in 2017. Under his leadership, Gatehouse launched the first PRS UK Residential Private Rental Scheme Fund with £100 million equity, and in 2021 sold the 918-unit Thistle Portfolio for circa £150 million, the largest transaction of its nature in the UK at the time.
achievements: Awarded US Army Commendation Medal for meritorious service during Operation Desert Storm
Co-founded Gatehouse Bank in 2007
Founded Gatehouse Capital KSCC in 2002
Recognized as top 40 under 40 real estate superstar by Real Estate Forum in 2011
Received Award for Business at Arabian Business London Awards in 2019
Led over US$1.25 billion in US Light Industrial investments
Pioneered UK's Build to Rent sector

figures.name: Nejoud Boodai
figures.title: Fashion Designer and Businesswoman
figures.roleDescription: Kuwaiti fashion designer and entrepreneur
figures.significance: Founded her own fashion house and launched Kuwait's first specialist cupcake bakery, pioneering various retail ventures in Kuwait.
figures.biography:
Nejoud Boodai was educated at the American College in London and founded her own fashion house, launching her first collection in 1995. She went on to open various boutique outlets in Kuwait, including N.B. Boutique in Salhiya Complex and a flower shop called Flower Story in 360 Mall. In 2007, she opened Juju's Cupcake, which was Kuwait's first specialist cupcake bakery, demonstrating her entrepreneurial innovation in the Kuwaiti retail market.

In 2008, she expanded her business ventures by opening Marble Slab Creamery in Al Kout Mall. Boodai died from cancer in August 2010, leaving behind a legacy of entrepreneurial achievement in Kuwait's fashion and retail sectors.
achievements: Founded own fashion house and launched first collection in 1995
Opened N.B. Boutique in Salhiya Complex
Established Kuwait's first specialist cupcake bakery (Juju's Cupcake) in 2007
Opened Flower Story flower shop in 360 Mall
Opened Marble Slab Creamery in Al Kout Mall in 2008

figures.name: Imad Bukhamseen
figures.title: Editor-in-Chief of Annahar
figures.roleDescription: Editor-in-Chief of Kuwaiti newspaper Annahar
figures.significance: Leading media figure in Kuwait as editor-in-chief of the Arabic newspaper Annahar.
figures.biography:
Imad Bukhamseen serves as the editor-in-chief of Annahar, an Arabic newspaper headquartered in Kuwait City, Kuwait. The newspaper was first published on September 2, 2007, becoming the ninth Arabic paper in the country, and is owned by the Dar Annahar for Press, Publishing and Distribution company, which is an affiliated body of Bukhamseen Holding.

Under his leadership, Annahar covers local and international political, art, culture and economic news. The daily is described as an independent political newspaper with a liberal leaning and had a circulation of 23,000 copies as of 2012. In March 2010, the newspaper faced legal challenges when it was fined 3,000 Kuwait dinars for publishing an article deemed offensive to the ruling Al Sabah family.
achievements: Editor-in-chief of major Kuwaiti newspaper
Oversees independent liberal media outlet

figures.name: Ramez Al-Khayyat
figures.title: President and GCEO of Power International Holding
figures.roleDescription: Qatari businessman and conglomerate leader
figures.significance: Leading Qatari businessman who heads one of the country's largest conglomerates and played a key role in Qatar's food security during the diplomatic crisis.
figures.biography:
Ramez Al-Khayyat is a Qatari businessman based in Doha, Qatar, who serves as President and GCEO of Power International Holding, a major Qatari conglomerate operating across general contracting, real estate, food and agriculture, hospitality, and services. He obtained his degree in Business from the University of the West of Scotland and earned a Certificate in Steering Complex Projects from the University of Cambridge.

Al-Khayyat began his career as a board member of Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading, a family business established by his father Mohamad Reslan Al-Khayyat in 1983. In 2011, he co-founded UCC Holding with his brother Moutaz Al-Khayyat, which became a large construction company in Qatar undertaking major projects including the Banana Island resort, Mall of Qatar, and Lekhwiya Sports Complex for the 2022 Football World Cup. He serves as Managing Director of Baladna Food Industries, Qatar's largest dairy producer providing 95% of the country's dairy products, which gained prominence during the Qatar diplomatic crisis by airlifting cows to bypass the blockade.

In 2016, Al-Khayyat received the Retail Leadership Award for the Mall of Qatar development. In April 2022, he was appointed Vice Chairman of Estithmar Holding QPSC and led a major reverse acquisition resulting in one of Qatar's largest conglomerates being listed on the Qatar Stock Exchange. In September 2022, Power International Holding was ranked 13th in Forbes Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses.
achievements: Co-founded UCC Holding in 2011
Led Baladna Food Industries to become Qatar's largest dairy producer
Received Retail Leadership Award in 2016 for Mall of Qatar
Power International Holding ranked 13th in Forbes Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses in 2022

figures.name: Moutaz Al-Khayyat
figures.title: Co-founder of UCC Holding and Baladna
figures.roleDescription: Syrian-Qatari businessman and co-founder of major Qatari companies
figures.significance: Syrian-Qatari businessman who co-founded major Qatari construction and agricultural companies including UCC Holding and Baladna Food Industries.
figures.biography:
Moutaz Al-Khayyat is a Syrian-Qatari businessman who has played a significant role in Qatar's construction and agricultural sectors. In 2011, he co-founded UCC Holding with his brother Ramez Al-Khayyat, establishing it as a large construction company in Qatar that has undertaken several major development projects including the Banana Island resort, the Mall of Qatar, and Lekhwiya Sports Complex, which was developed as part of the 2022 Football World Cup infrastructure.

Al-Khayyat also co-founded Baladna, a Qatari agricultural company that raises livestock and produces dairy products, which became Qatar's largest locally owned food and dairy producer, supplying over 85% of the country's fresh dairy products. Baladna is part of Power International Holding and gained particular prominence during the June 2017 Qatar diplomatic crisis when it rapidly expanded to produce cow's milk that was previously supplied primarily by Saudi Arabia, helping to ensure Qatar's food security during the blockade.
achievements: Co-founded UCC Holding in 2011
Co-founded Baladna agricultural company
Contributed to Qatar's food security during the 2017 diplomatic crisis

figures.name: Mohamad Reslan Al-Khayyat
figures.title: Founder of Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the Al-Khayyat family business empire
figures.significance: Established the foundational family business in 1983 that would later grow into a major Qatari business conglomerate.
figures.biography:
Mohamad Reslan Al-Khayyat was the patriarch who established the Al-Khayyat family's business presence in Qatar. In 1983, he founded Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading, which became the foundational family business from which the Al-Khayyat business empire would grow.

His sons Ramez Al-Khayyat and Moutaz Al-Khayyat would later build upon this foundation, with Ramez starting his career as a board member of the family company before expanding into larger ventures. The business legacy established by Mohamad Reslan Al-Khayyat eventually grew into Power International Holding, one of Qatar's major conglomerates operating across multiple sectors including construction, real estate, food and agriculture, hospitality, and services.
achievements: Founded Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading in 1983

figures.name: Yousif bin Abdulrahman Fakhro
figures.title: Founder of Fakhro Group
figures.roleDescription: Merchant and business founder
figures.significance: Founded the Fakhro Group in 1888, establishing one of Bahrain's oldest and most prominent family businesses.
figures.biography:
Yousif bin Abdulrahman Fakhro founded the Fakhro Group in 1888, starting as a traditional merchant house. He remained at the helm of the business from the early 20th century until his death in 1952. During the 1930s and 1940s, Yousif strategically sent each of his five sons to manage the family's business interests across different regions including India, Dubai, Bahrain and Iraq, where Abdulla bin Yousif Fakhro was based for numerous years.

During his leadership, the small business dealing in dates and building materials expanded significantly into shipping, operating around 40 dhows, and established branches across the GCC, Iraq, and India.
